condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

1.1: Extent to which manufacturers have the capacities to produce Ozone friendly appliances/products Scale: 1. Capacity building of stakeholders to limited extent (1-25%) 2. Capacity building of stakeholders to reasonable extent (26-50%) 3. Capacity building of stakeholders to large extent (51-100%)
1.2: Extent to which policy regulation/guidelines at the federal and provincial level exist to ensure introduction of usage of clean technology for the protection of ozone layer
Scale: 1. No policy regulation/guidelines both at the federal and provincial level exist (0%) 2. policy regulation/guidelines at provincial level exist (1-50%); [Desc.: If two provinces then 25% and if four provinces then 50%] 3. policy regulation/guidelines at both federal and provincial level (51-100%)
1.3: Number of eligible importers/manufacturers receive allocated quota to import Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS)
1.4: Number of import data reports, generated by FBR reviewed and compiled by National Ozone Unit, are submitted to MLF

comment.narrative: Baseline 1.1: Scale item 2 Baseline 1.2: Scale item 2 Baseline 1.3: 26 [21 are total quota holders who are allocated quota on yearly basis] Baseline 1.4: 2

1.1: Scale 3 [Maximum industry and end users will be aware of the advantages of new technology and its impact towards Ozone protection and climate mitigation] 1.2: Scale 3 [51-100% policy regulation/guidelines at both federal and provincial level exist] 1.3: 26 [Quota to 26 eligible HCFC quota holder was allocated on yearly basis] 1.4: 2 [2 Import data reports analyzed to ensure compliance to Montreal Protocol]

1.1: Scale 2 [industry and end users are well aware of the advantages of new technology and its impact towards Ozone protection and climate mitigation. CFCs has been totally eliminated while the HCFCs based industries are being covered to HFCs and / or HCs to protect the fragile ozone layer] 1.2: Scale 2 [2. policy regulation/guidelines at provincial level exist (1-50%); (Desc.: If two provinces then 25% and if four provinces then 50%)] 1.3: 21 [Quota to 21 eligible HCFC quota holder was allocated on yearly basis] 1.4: 12 [12 Import data reports analyzed to ensure compliance to Montreal Protocol. 2 reports i.e. A7 Data Report and Country Programme Reports are submitted to MLFS and Ozone Secretariat to remain in compliance with regard to the provisions of the Montreal Protocol]

1.1: Scale 3 No target 1.2: Scale 3 [policy regulation/guidelines at both federal and provincial level (51-100%)] 1.3: 26 [21 are total quota holders who are allocated quota on yearly basis] 1.4: 2 [2 Import data reports to be thoroughly analyzed to ensure compliance to the Montreal Protocol]

1.1: Scale 3 No result 1.2: Scale 3 [no progress] 1.3: 26 [no progress] 1.4: 2 [no progress]
Note: Submission on update of Article 07 by National Ozone Unit. Due to change in implementation modality, the project team was laid off with request of recruitment of new team through UNDP. The recruitment process is in final stages.

description.narrative: TUNDP has a global mandate to support governments in strengthening security and justice sector governance) and has extensive experience in proving strategic advice and assistance in this regard in a range of countries and contexts globally. description.narrative: 1.1: Extent to which the ECP's institutional capacity is enhanced 1.2: Extent to which the ECP delivers improved electoral administration. 1.3: Extent to which the ECP engages constructively with stakeholders especially women. 2.1: Extent to which legislative institutions actively engage with the SDGs/development processes 2.2: Extent to which the technical skills of legislators and Secretariat staff are enhanced. 2.3: Extent to which parliamentary institutions are prepared to function during crisis/emergency situation 3.1: Number of citizens reached through civic literacy initiatives (m/f) 4.1: Number of case studies produced by journalist trained under training for media professionals

1.1 3: Adequate Capacity (Adequate processes/capacity development plan/training modules/trained staff exist) 1.2 3: Adequate (Adequate administrative processes/training plans/training modules/trained staff exist) 1.3 3: Moderately (Engagement strategies/mechanicisms exist and implemented, Gender strategy/gender-centric initiatives exit and implemented) 2.1 3: Partially (Basic understanding of SDGs and partial engagement with SDGs/development processes) 2.2 3: Partially: Basic understanding of legislative mechanisms/processes 2.3: 3 : Partial: Basic awareness and preparedness 3.1 16,000 (M: 12,000, F: 4,000) 4.1 0 training session

1.1. Scale 3: UNDP/SELP enhanced the ECP’ institutional capacity by providing support for the development of a) mandate, scope and framework for the Federal Election Academy (FEA) b) trained 32 ECP officials on ‘Activity Planning’, developed finalized activity plans at the wing level for effective implementation of ECP’s Strategic Plan and developed monitoring tools for activities c) trained 27 ECP officials on Protection against Harassment of Women at the Workplace Act 2010 d) reached out to over 92 million Pakistanis for a voter verification process e) published a legally mandated Annual Report to Parliament f) held discussions on the development of a Gender Mainstreaming and Social Inclusion Framework
1.2. Scale 3: (the prerequisite for the electoral events are complete). However, the electoral administrationn will take place during the local government elections, which are yet to be held next year): UNDP/SELP supported the ECP a) to develop training materials for Boundary Delimitation in Punjab, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Sindh and for the Local Government Elections in Punjab and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) and for the Cantonment Board Elections b) to train 21 ECP officials at FEA for the boundary delimitation process in Punjab, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Sindh and 229 delimitation committee members of Punjab and KP c) to train 47 officials for the LG elections in Punjab to carry out their election duties d) to develop Sops for LG electoral process.In addition, UNDP provided support to the Punjab Local Government Department in the preparation of rules for the upcoming LG elections. 1.3. Scale 3: (partially achieved as due to covid 19 NADRA offices were closed and their registration vans were grounded. The gender mainstreaming strategy has been initiated and is in progress): The project continued to support the nationwide joint ECP-NADRA Womens NIC and Voter Registration Campaign to reduce the gender gap between males and females on the electoral roll. UNDP/SELP supported the ECPs implemention of the Campaign in partnership with Trust for Democractic Education and Accountability in 85 districts, which has a network of member organizations across Pakistan. UNDP/SELP registered 132,715 women for their NICs and to become voters. UNDP/SELP ensured ECPs constructive engagement with stakeholders, and partners at the national, provincial and districts levels. UNDP/SELP in collaboration with TDEA also conducted a media campaign to the support this campaign.Additionally, UNDP/SELP held discussions with the ECPs Gender unit for the development of a Gender Mainstreaming and Social Inclusion Framework.
2.1.Scale 3: The project mobilized lawmakers of Balochistan and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) Assemblies throuh establishing and supporting SDGs Task Forces. With UNDP/SELP support, the MPs established linkages with different departments of the provincial governments and as a result, multiple briefing sessions were (3 in the reporting year) held in which 12 senior officials of relevant branches of the executive were invited to brief the lawmakers about the progress on various SDG-related schemes/projects in addition to explaining the challenges for the lawmakers could take up the matter in the Assembly session to address those. 2.2. Scale 3: UNDP/SELP organised various trainings and capacity-building events for lawmakers and Secretariat staff of the Balochistan and KP Assemblies with the focus to enhance their technical skills to enable them to discharge their functions more effectively and efficiently. As a result of such interventions, the Balochistan Assembly Public Accounts Committee (PAC) has been able to lay its first ever report before the House. The unprecedented feat has been achieved after two UNDP/SELP-organised trainings to the staff and Members of the PAC. with 42 participants. Apart from these initiatives, training of trainers, workshop on strengthening of committees, and effective media engagement were also undertaken. 2.3. Scale 3: UNDP/SELP is conducting Business Continuity Assessments studies of the Senate, and the Balochistan and KP Assemblies with a view to enabling the legislative houses to identify issues and challenges related to the legal framework, administrative and IT infrastructure and capacity to function during the times of crisis. Similarly, the project is also developing two separate Guides on the oversight role of committees for Balochistan and KP Assemblies to enable the targeted committees to continue functioning during times of crises such as Covid-19. To this regard, the draft frameworks and consultations with the Secretarit and political leadership of respective assemblies are completed. The project has also provided online tools (in the form of 16 Zoom software licenses) to Balochistan (06 licenses) and KP (05 lincenses) Assemblies and PIPS (05 licenses) to continue working remotely during Covid-19. UNDP/SELP is providing the associated training on the use of technology to hold virtual sessions. The partner institutions have already started using the software tools for holding meetings and organising remote trainings. 3.1. Yes-UNDP SELP recruited United Nations Youth Volunteers in each of Paksitans four provinces to conduct voter and civic education activites at the rural grassroots level. These UNVs conducted 947 civic awareness initaitives, reaching 425,247 people (F: 17,345, M: 21,151, T: 34, Misc: 386,717) through direct and online platforms. The project continued to support the joint ECP-NADRA Womens NIC and Voter Registration Campaign to reduce the gender gap on the electoral roll. Over 95,286 individuals were directly reached through 3,586 stakeholder and mobilization activities. The media Campaign through Radio and Television reached out to 86.2 miilion people. This UNDP support attributed to the registration of over 1.8 million women.Additionally, to support the ECP for the voter verification process an outreach campaign through radio and television reached out to 92.25 million people. 4.1. No Target for the year 2020

1.1 Scale Item 3: Adequate Capacity (Adequate processes/capacity development plan/training modules/trained staff exist) 1.2 Scale Item 3: Adequate (Adequate administrative processes/training plans/training modules/trained staff exist) 1.3 No target. 2.1 No target. 2.2 No target. 2.3 Scale Item 3: Partial: Basic awareness and preparedness 3.1 18,000 (M:13,000, F:5,000) 4.1 No target.

1.1. Scale 3: UNDP/SELP provided technical assistance and quality assurance to the ECP for the development of its legally mandated Annual Report to Parliament submitted in March 2021. UNDP/SELP supported the ECP in the development and printing of outreach materials related to COVID-19 SOPs for bye elections. UNDP/SELP provided 130 banners and 450 posters, and it is estimated that the number of voters reached through this material was 69,500 voters of which 35,445 were men and 34,055 were women. UNDP/SELP supported the ECP to develop a public outreach video message for International Women’s Day (IWD) by the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC), which was uploaded on the ECP’s website, Facebook page and included as news tickers on key television channels. In his message, the CEC reiterated ECP’s commitment for inclusive electoral processes for women and all segments of society, including minorities, persons with disabilities and transgenders.UNDP/SELP is providing ongoing technical support to the ECP for a pre-service course for newly recruited election officers. This support is with respect to development of materials, detailed training and execution plans, quality assurance, monitoring/evaluation tools and facilitation during the upcoming course.UNDP/SELP revised and shared the draft Women’s NIC and Voter Registration Campaign Phase IV continuation document for the sustainability and continuation of the ECP-NADRA Campaign, while taking into account lessons learned and recommendations from previous phases of the Campaign.
1.2 Scale 3: UNDP led technical assistance to the ECP as related to its implementation of the Senate elections at the Federal and Provincial levels. With the support of UNDP/SELP, the ECP developed a Senate Election Handbook for election officials, and a guide for the members of parliament and provincial assemblies. Additionally, UNDP/SELP provided technical assistance to the ECP to successfully conduct two 3-day trainings for ROs and Polling Officers with a pool of 46 ECP’s officers (M:42, F:04) and a pre-election refresher training with a trained pool of 31 ROs and Polling Officers (M: 29, F:2). UNDP/SELP provided technical assistance to the ECP in the planning and production of training materials for the trainings of delimitation committees and election officials as related to local government and cantonment board elections. UNDP/SELP supported the Local Government Department-Punjab and Provincial Election Commission-Punjab in the drafting of the local government election rules in line with the revised legal framework. 1.3: No target for the year 2.1: No target for the year 2.2: No target for the year 2.3 Scale 3( UNDP/SELP conducted Business Continuity Assessments studies of the Senate, and the Balochistan and KP Assemblies with a view to enabling the legislative houses to identify issues and challenges related to the legal framework, administrative and IT infrastructure and capacity to function during the times of crisis. Similarly, the project developed two separate Guides on the oversight role of committees for Balochistan and KP Assemblies to enable the targeted committees to continue functioning during times of crises such as Covid-19. Additionally, UNDP/SELP provided the associated trainings on the use of technology to hold virtual sessions. 3.1 18,000 (M:13000, M: 5,000)(UNDP/SELP supported the ECP in the development and printing of outreach materials related to COVID-19 SOPs for bye elections. UNDP/SELP provided 130 banners and 450 posters, and it is estimated that the number of voters reached through this material was 69,500 voters of which 35,445 were men and 34,055 were women. 4.1: No target for the year

1.1 Scale 3: Capacity (Adequate processes/capacity development plan/training modules/trained staff exist) 1.2 Scale 3: Adequate administrative processes/training plans [description: technical and operational support to electoral events for election officials and LG department. Multiple stakeholder dialogues on electoral reforms] 1.3 Scale 3: (Engagement strategies/mechanisms exist and implemented, Gender strategy/gender-centric initiatives exit and implemented) 2.1 No target. 2.2 No target. 2.3 No target 3.1 – 25,000 (M:15,000, F:10,000) sessions with youth champions from universities and colleges to enhance participation of youth and other marginalized groups in electoral processes. 4.1 – No target.

1.1 Scale 3: UNDP/SELP support for the ECP led to the a) Capacity development of 1,377 (1,160 men and 217 women) ECP, election, law, gender, IT and media and communication officers, election officials, lead trainers and master trainers. b) Introduced digital (beta version) M&E system for ECP with customized data tools (pilot testing). In addition, capacity building of 26 (3 women and 23 men) ECP Officers as Master Trainers-cum-Compliance Monitors for piloting this system was carried out. c) Development of ECP’s research, R&D structure, and staffing plan; developing ECP’s draft roadmap for internet voting system (IVS) and ECP’s networking with international elections technology experts to obtain advice and inputs on IVS.
1.2. Scale 3: a) Consultations on reforms in Election Act 2017, amendment bill 2021, and on Effective Local Governments led to enhanced coordination among the electoral/political stakeholders such as political parties, civil society, and media , taking perspectives to strengthen the electoral processes in Pakistan. b) Consultation on political finance led key electoral stakeholders including political parties, media, civil society, academia, and ECP to discuss the system of political finance in Pakistan, and the reforms required.
1.3. Scale 3: a) The Project supported several initiatives of ECP to expand its outreach and engagement with electoral stakeholders, including International Day of Democracy, International Womens Day. b) Project support to the ECP on voter education for local government elections Sindh which contributed to the informed and inclusive electoral process in the province. In voter education campaigns women were the main focus and approximately 3,004,344 women were reached through digital and social media campaigns, while 1,687 were female students who attended in-person voter education sessions. c) The project supported the ECP in enhancing the required technical and professional skills of 217 women election officials and ECP officers for ECP’s improved electoral administration and management. d) To reinforce the Phase IV of the Women’s NIC and Voter Registration Campaign success, UNDP continued its support and advocacy to the ECP, NADRA, and Campaign implementation partners to hold regular coordination meetings. UNDP circulated action points at these meetings for discussion for problem-solving and ultimately increasing NIC/VR registration.
2.1 No result 2.2 No result 2.3 No result.

description.narrative:
Language updated after Pro-Doc Revision in 2019
1.1: Extent to which federal institutions develop and effectively implement gender sensitive and inclusive policies/regulations ensuring protection and promotion of human rights. Scale: 1: Policies/regulations partially developed. 2: Policies/regulations developed but not implemented. 3: Policies/regulations developed and partially implemented
1.2: Extent to which technical capacity of federal institutions is enhanced to enable duty-bearers to effectively deliver on their respective mandates, particularly for women and youth empowerment Scale: (1: Very Basic Capacity 2: Partial Capacity 3: Capacity Largely in Place)
2.1: Existence of strengthened institutions and systems supporting fulfilment of nationally and internationally ratified human rights obligations: a) Rule of law and justice b) human rights Scale 1: Policies/regulations partially developed. 2: Policies/regulations developed but not implemented. 3: Policies/regulations developed and partially implemented
2.2: Technical capacity of provincial institutions on human rights and local governance is enhanced Scale:1: Very Basic Capacity 2: Partial Capacity 3: Capacity Largely in Place
3.1: Extent to which LGs are capacitated for provision of inclusive (particularly women) service delivery. Scale: 1: Very Partial Capacity 2: Partial capacity 3: Capacity Largely in Place
4.1: Extent to which citizens oversight enables improved sectoral government action 1: Limited Capacity 2: Very Partial Capacity 3: Partial capacity 4. Capacity largely exist

comment.narrative: 1.1: Scale 1 (No capacity) 1.2: Scale item 1: Very Basic Capacity 2.1: Scale 1 (No capacity) (Technical support provided to KP Law Dept. workshops & trainings on HR protection mechanisms) 3.1: Scale 1 (No capacity) (Cells established in KP only but LGs not operationalized) 4.1: Scale 1 (Not at all)

1.1: Scale item 2: Policies/regulations developed but not implemented (National Action Plan on business & Human Rights developed and implementation initiated; NCHR work plan developed & 25% recommendations implemented) 1.2: Scale item 2: Partial Capacity(Human Rights Information Management System operationalized; Inter-Provincial Working Group operationalized) 2.1: Scale Item 2: Policies/regulations developed but not implemented (Support for development/implementation of provinci al LG and HR policies/plans) 2.2: Scale Item 1: Very Basic Capacity(Provincial data systems developed in all four provinces; staff trained on data entry and reporting; LG staff trained on inclusive service delivery in KP and Balochistan) 3.1: Scale Item 2: Partial capacity (Institutional digitization programme for inclusive, transparent and accountable service delivery designed for target district in all four provinces; Psycho social support and economic empowerment programme rolled out for 3000 (50% women) vulnerable and at-risk youth in all four provinces 4.1: Scale Item 2: Very Partial Capacity(Integration Support Groups (ISGs) assisting Social Welfare Departments in 3 target districts each in Sindh, KP and Punjab; partially improved engagement between CSOs and government for improved access to services of the most vulnerable and at-risk populations (particularly women, transgenders and youth)

1.1: Scale item 2: Policies/regulations developed but not implemented-Nationwide consultations conducted with government, civil society and private sector stakeholders for development of draft National Action Plan; continued support provided to NCHR to implement 25% recommendations as per 2 year work plan. 1.2: Scale item 2: Partial Capacity-HRIMS servers installed in all four provinces; linkages established with federal HRIMS 2.1: Scale Item 2: Yes, Support provided to HR, LG and Social Welfare departments; however delays due to COVID-19 in implementation of provincial plans. 2.2: Scale Item 1: Very Basic Capacity development exercises conducted in KP for HRIMS staff; LG and SWD staff training on inclusive service delivery (twinning) and gender-based planning and budgeting 3.1:Scale Item 2: Partial capacity- COVID-19 task established to develop a road-map for inclusive local governance in line with the UN’s soc-economic assessment; psycho-social support and economic empowerment programme implemented in Swat and Multan; gender desks established in NMAs, Swat, Multan and Karachi 4.1:Scale Item 2: Very Partial Capacity: ISGs established and linked with local administration for increased awareness and oversight of development initiatives; helplines for psycho-social support to vulnerable and at-risk populations established in partnership with local government and civil society actors

1.1: Scale item 3: Policies/regulations developed and partially implemented (National Action Plan approved by competent authority and implementation of short-term recommendations initiated; support provided to NHRIs, including NCHR, NCRC, NCSW; technical assistance provided to implement policies targeting vulnerable groups, particularly persons with disabilities) 1.2: Scale item 2: Partial Capacity(Human Rights Information Management System operationalized; Inter-Provincial Working Group operationalized) 2.1: Scale Item 2: Policies/regulations developed but not implemented (Support provided for development/implementation of provincial LG and HR policies/plans) 2.2: Scale Item 2: Partial Capacity (Provincial HRIMS stakeholders provided capacity development support; Social inclusion and social protection measures (helplines, shelter homes, referral mechanisms) established in all four provinces; institutionalization of protection measures in partnership with LG and SW Departments) 3.1: Scale Item 2: Partial capacity (Implementation of short-term recommendations of the socioeconomic assessment and COVID-19 task-force recommendations; programme on gender-justice and inclusion of persons with disabilities, and transgenders rolled-out; community stabilization programme implemented in Balochistan and discussions with government stakeholders on institutional of the model at the local level initiated) 4.1: Scale Item 2: Very Partial Capacity(Integration Support Groups (ISGs) assisting Social Welfare Departments in 3 target districts each in Sindh, KP and Punjab; partially improved engagement between CSOs and government for improved access to services of the most vulnerable and at-risk populations (particularly women, transgenders and youth)

Inter-provincial working group on human rights established 2.1: Scale Item 2: Yes, Support provided to HR, LG and Social Welfare Departments; however, delays due to COVID-19 in implementation of provincial plans. 2.2: Scale Item 1: Very Basic Capacity development exercises conducted in Sindh, Balochistan, Punjab and KP for HRIMS staff; LG and SWD staff training on inclusive service delivery (twinning) and gender-based planning and budgeting. Transgender centre established in Islamabad. 3.1: Scale Item 2: Partial capacity- COVID-19 taskforce initiated implementation for inclusive response to Covid-19 in line with the UN’s soc-economic assessment; psycho-social support and economic empowerment programme implemented in Swat and Multan; gender desks established in NMAs, Swat, Multan and Karachi 4.1: Scale Item 2: Very Partial Capacity: ISGs established and linked with local administration for increased awareness and oversight of development initiatives; helplines for psycho-social support to vulnerable and at-risk populations established in partnership with local government and civil society actors; implementation of LLDR model initiated in Quetta

description.narrative:
1.1: Extent to which performance tracking mechanism is in place and functional Scale: 1: not functional 2: very partially functional 3: partially functional 4: Functional
1.2: Extent to which resources are available and effectively deliver on performance targets Scale: 1 no capacity 2 very partial capacity 3 partial capacity 4 capacity largely in place
1.3: Extent to which government applies digital / electronic measures to improve its functioning. Scale 1: not at all 2: very partially 3: partially 4: largely
1.4: Extent to which institutions show innovation and citizen-centeredness Scale 1: not at all 2: Partially for I but not C 3: Partially for C but not I 4: Partially for both 5: Largely for I but not C 6: Largely for C but not I 7: Largely for both
2.1: The extent to which research and analysis informs the implementation of reforms Scale 1. No change 2. To a small extent 3. To some extent 4. To a great extent 5. Fully evident
2.2: Reforms are guided and supported by stakeholders Scale 1: not at all 2: very partially 3: partially 4: largely
2.3: Number of research and pilots that support improvement in Public Sector Delivery Scale 1: No study or pilot 2: at least 1 study or pilot 3: 2 studies or 2 pilots 4: four studies 5: five studies
4.1: The extent to which MOPDR leads and monitors other ministries/partners on reform and innovation Scale 1. No change 2. To a small extent 3. To some extent 4. To a great extent 5. Fully evident
4.2: Extent to which a positive debate and understanding on governance reforms exists Scale 1. No change 2. To a small extent 3. To some extent 4. To a great extent 5. Fully evident
4.3: Types of knowledge created and shared (a. participation in events, b. published research, c. news reports) and used to influence governance reforms. Scale 1: none 2: one of the three types 3: two of the three types 4: all three types 5: over and above the three types

comment.narrative: 1.1: Scale 2 (very partially functional) (No performance-based management system exists in government institutions. Performance reports (ACR/PERs) are used but they are not effectively tied to performance) 1.2: Scale 2 (very partial capacity) (Ministries have limited capacity in place to effectively manage performance-centered reforms) 1.3: Scale 2 (very partially) (Only limited digital mechanisms for government transparency, accountability and responsiveness exist such as e-filing in Establishment Division) 1.4: Scale 1 (not at all) (Public service delivery is very conventional and the perception of government’s public service delivery is poor and there is little motivation or incentive to improve or recognition of innovation) 2.1: Scale 2 (to a small extent) (Research exists, such as the NCGR report and previous Pay Commission reports, but are rarely used to inform the way of work) 2.2: Scale 2 (very partially) (There is general agreement that the civil service needs improvement but there is considerable skepticism about the reform effort, lack of clarity on what is to be done, and wide difference about how to go about it) 2.3: Scale 1 (No study) (There is a general consensus that public sector service delivery need to be improved. However, there is not much information on evidence of the type of governance reforms needed to achieve this) 4.1: Scale 2 (to a small extent) (Insufficient human resource capacities, systems, procedures and MOPDR hamper its performance) 4.2: Scale 2 (to a small extent) (There is general support but incomplete understanding of reforms as shown through media reports and social media feedback) 4.3: Scale 3 (two of the three types) (Research is created but only has limited dissemination, use and influence on policy)

1.1: 3- Partially functional 1.2: 3-Partial capacity 1.3: 2- Very Partially- No Target 1.4: 1-4 2.1: 4- To a great extent 2.2: 3- Partially 2.3: 2- At least 1 study or pilot 4.1: 3- To some extent 4.2: 3- To some extent 4.3: 4- All three types

1.1: 3- (Partially functional)-Performance contracts signed by the Prime Minister with relevant ministries-The Government has signed 11 performance contracts on pilot basis. Under R&I’s ongoing support for Management Services (MS) Wing at the Establishment Division, a comprehensive capacity assessment is being undertaken to identify institutional and human resource gaps in the current set up. 1.2: 2-(Partial capacity)-The project is undertaking development of case studies of service delivery initiatives in KP and Punjab, with support by FCDO, to document best practices and lessons learnt for future initiatives. The case studies are in progress and will be completed in 2021, hence an achievement rating of 2. 1.3: 2- (Very Partially)-.Supporting Federal Government (e.g. Prime Minister Office, Establishment Division, Federal Public Services Commission, Auditor General of Pakistan, Accountant General Pakistan Revenues) to install wide-scale restructuring and e-governance measures to improve transparency, accountability and responsiveness. The project is conducting an all-encompassing digital readiness assessment of the Ministry of Information Technology & Telecommunication (MoITT) and its key attached organizations: National Information technology Board, IGNITE, and National Telecommunication Corporation. This assessment will gauge organizational digital readiness in three categories: a) infrastructure maturity b) business process continuity c) digital readiness of employees. 1.4:0 (The project had planned to organize Innovation Awards / Fund / Policy and National Consultation on Governance reforms / models. However, the activity could not be implemented on account of government priorities.) 2.1: 4- The project actively contributed actionable research towards implementation of reforms. The Terms of Reference for conducting studies on Civil Service / Service Delivery Reforms (Recruitment, Trainings, Job Descriptions, Performance Management, Tenure Protection, and Compensation were prepared and shared with the donor and the government.
Anti-corruption / integrity module for training of probationary officers at the Civil Services Academy (CSA) introduced. The objective of this module is to inculcate a sense of integrity and accountability into the mindset of new officers right at the start of their careers 2.2: 3- (Partially)-The project actively liaised with all key stakeholder groups (including the Task Forces on Civil Service Reforms and Austerity, CCIR and the MoPDSI) to facilitate a consultation-based reform effort.
2.3: 2- Citizen Perception Survey initiated in KP and Punjab through support by the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO). The survey will identify areas and districts where citizens’ satisfaction with service delivery is low and identify underlying factors which negatively affect service delivery.
4.1: 3- (To some extent)-Provided technical assistance, on need-basis, to MoPDSI to act as a research think-tank to Government of Pakistan. R&I is also supporting the Cabinet Committee on Institutional Reforms towards developing a consensus on key reform efforts being introduced by the Governmen 4.2: 3- (To some extent)-The project had planned for concept development and launch of a Smart Government Institute (Pilot) and Governance Innovation App Store (Pilot). The activity could not be implemented due to change in government priorities. However, the project actively engaged with the PMs Task Forces on Civil Service Reforms and Austerity, CCIR and the MoPDSI which are taking a lead in civil service reform debate in Pakistan.
4.3: 4- The project had planned for organizing governance reform conference in Pakistan during the reporting year. The initiative could not be implemented due to the COVID’19 restrictions. However, extensive consultations held with CCIR, MoPDSI and the Task Forces to support the government’s reform agenda.

1.1: 3- Partially functional 1.2: 3-Partial capacity 1.3: 2 - Very Partially- No Target 1.4: Partially for both 2.1: 4- To a great extent 2.2: 3- Partially 2.3: 2- At least 1 study or pilot 4.1: 3- To some extent 4.2: 3- To some extent 4.3: 4- All three types

1.1- No Results achieved- The AWP activities were planned for Q3 & 4, and might get changed as per instructions of the Government. 1.2. Scale 2 Two case studies focused on the Sub-National Governance targeted themes developed. 1.3. No Results- Activity not initiated. 1.4. Scale 4. Partially for both- The AWP activities were planned for Q3 & 4, and might get changed as per instructions of the Government 2.1. Scale Item 3. The Compensation Study has been submitted by PIDE. Another study on vacant positions in PSEs under process. 2.2. Scale item 3. The project was formally designated as a support project to Cabinet Committee on Institutional reforms (CCIR) to facilitate a consultation-based reform effort. 2.3. Scale item 2. Citizen Perception Survey initiated in KP and Punjab through support by the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO). First wave of the survey completed. output 3 not functional 4.1. Scale item 3. Provided technical assistance, on need-basis, to MoPDSI to act as a research think-tank to Government of Pakistan. R&I is also supporting the Cabinet Committee on Institutional Reforms towards developing a consensus on key reform efforts being introduced by the Government. 4.2. No Results- Activity not initiated. 4.3. Scale Item 2. The project created an extensive knowledge and research base which is supporting innovation and reforms in governance and public service delivery at the MoPDSI during the previous years

1.1: Scale 3 (Government institutions are regularly and effectively tracking and managing their performance) 1.2: Scale 3 (Ministries are able to utilize their resources to improve performance) 1.3: Scale 3 (Extent to which government applies digital / electronic measures to improve its functioning) 1.4: Scale 1-4 (Government institutions show innovation and citizen-centeredness in public service delivery) 2.1: Scale 4 (High quality research is produced and it informs the reforms process) 2.2: Scale 3 (Stakeholders understand and agree to a broad range of reforms and endorse the implementation strategy 2.3 :Scale 2 (at least 1 study or pilot) (evidence exists on how government reforms can improve public sector service delivery in selected sections) 3.1:1- No target 3.2:2- No target 3.3:3- No target 4.1: Scale 3 (MOPDSI is recognized as a model ministry capable of leading change) 4.2: Scale 3 (Greater understanding and support for reforms amongst the general public) 4.3: Scale 4 (All types of research, learning and sharing is done and used to influence policy and reforms)

1.1: Scale 3 [The project provided support to the Office of Special Assistant to Prime Minister on Establishment for implementation of Performance Contracts signed by the Prime Minister with Federal ministries and divisions. Strategic communications support provided to PMO for development of communications strategy, and organizing Performance Contracts Awards Ceremony, along with development of communications collateral. Strengthened capacity of Prime Minister’s Delivery Unit by identifying areas and sectors which are constantly failing to perform at the required levels, and proposing concrete remedial measures for enhancing performance]. 1.2: Scale 3 [Provided technical assistance to all ministries and divisions (41) through a team of support consultants aimed at capacity development for implementation of performance contracts]. 1.3: Scale 3 [Digitization of performance agreements through National Information Technology Board (NITB) was carried out. The end product was an enhanced Performance Agreements Portal which interfaces with the users (ministries) and supports the Peer Review Committee (PRC), responsible for the final clearance on the agreements. Provided technical and operational support to Pakistan Customs for development and launch of an Online Complaint Portal for facilitating traders, especially the young entrepreneurs, in hassle-free lodging of complaints – thus promoting ease of doing business and improving governance outcomes by enabling digital interventions]. 1.4: Scale 4 Partially for both 2.1: 4 [Commissioned an ‘Executive Recruitment in Public Sector Organizations’ study as a diagnostic exercise to provide evidence and reasons behind delays in the recruitment process for top-level vacancies in public sector organizations in Pakistan. The study provides data on 68 public sector organizations; analyses laws governing the hiring process, and multiple challenges faced by public sector organizations in hiring for senior positions. Based on this review, a set of recommendations grounded in international case studies and best practices followed by public and private sectors were proposed to the government. Subsequent to the above-mentioned study, the project was requested to support the MoPDSI in development of a Recruitment Manual for Executive Level Hiring encompassing a review of the legal framework governing the recruitment processes of public sector organizations, identify hinderances in fast-track recruitment and propose concrete ways to improve the recruitment strategy through amendments in the relevant rules. The study will help the government in streamlining recruitment processes for senior positions on contract basis, and fast-tracking recruitment through documenting HR policies, procedures, and rules]. The project also conducted a study to comprehensively review the civil service salary structures in Pakistan. The study titled ‘Civil Service Compensation: Incentives, Dissatisfaction and Costs’ was conducted in partnership with Pakistan Institute of Development Economics (PIDE). A comprehensive review and updation of the ‘Manual for Development Projects’ was carried out in response to a request by MoPDSI. The last edition of the Manual was prepared in 1997, followed by the ‘Guidelines for Project Management’ published in 2008. During a span of over 12 years, numerous changes were introduced in the processes and procedures of development projects, which warranted fresh documentation]. 2.2:0 – [Activity cancelled due to change in government priorities.] 2.3: 2 [Completed two rounds of an extensive three-phase survey to measure citizens’ satisfaction with public service delivery in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Punjab. The survey covers access and acceptability of public services including WASH, health, education and citizens’ engagement and awareness, with a sample of 27,720 households.] 3.1:1- No result 3.2:2- No result 3.3:3- No result 4.1: scale 3 [ Strengthened MoPDSI’s capacity as a think-tank to Government of Pakistan through development of a comprehensive Technical Assistance (TA) plan to help target high impact areas of MoPDSI to ensure a stronger integration and internalization of reforms. The TA plan addresses four priority areas including M&E capabilities, policy evaluation function, development of strategic documents and business process refinement]. 4.2: 0 [Activity cancelled due to change in government priorities.] 4.3: 2 [The project created an extensive knowledge and research base which is supporting innovation and reforms in governance and public service delivery at the MoPDSI during the previous years. The current year, however, was spent more on responding to the new demands on ground due to the change in government.]

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative: 1.1 Extent to which institutional frameworks are implaced for integration of conservation, Sustainable Natural Resource use, controlling management of wildlife crime and illegal wildlife trade and improved livelihoods into landscape planning and management 1.2. Area of snow leopard habitat under integrated landscapes management 1.3 Status of snow leopard and associated endangered species populations in the landscapes [Scale 1 Low (0-1%), Scale 2 Medium (2-5%), Scale 3 High (6-8%) and Scale 4 Very high (8-10%) 1.4: Level of institutional capacity of provincial government agencies for planning, implementation and monitoring integrated landscape plans as measured by UNDP capacity development scorecard [Scale 1- Low (0-25%), Scale 2 Medium (26-50%), Scale 3 High (>50%)] 2.1 Effectiveness in management of protected areas within landscape as measured by Management Effectiveness Tracking Tool 2.2 Number of management and financing plans for protected areas with adequate financial resources developed and approved by Provincial government 2.3 Number of forest and wildlife staff and community members actively engaged in wildlife crime monitoring and surveillance to reduce incidence of wildlife crime 3.1 Number of Valley conservation plans developed and under implementation 3.2: Average increase in income of communities from Sustainable livelihood and resource management activities Scale 1: Less than 10,000 PKR, Scale 2: More than 10,000 PKR 3.3 Decrease incidences of human-wildlife conflict as indicated by:(i) Reduce incidence of retaliatory killing of snow leopard and other predators 4.1 Increase in the level of awareness of communities in the target landscapes on conservation and sustainable use and threats to snow leopard and biodiversity [Scale: 1= Not adequately (up to 25%); 2= Very partially (26%-50%); 3= Partially (51%-75%); 4= Largely (75%-100%)] 4.2: Number of knowledge products reflecting best practices and lessons learnt documented, disseminated and up sca

comment.narrative: 1.1 Scale 1 1.2. Scale 1 (10,000 [hectares currently managed effectively in protected areas]) 1.3 Scale 1 {200 [Snow leopard population estimate at + 200 individuals in Northern Pakistan with associated species baselines as: Karakoram-Pamir LS:, Himalayan Ibex-4,900; Markhor -230; Blue Sheep – 750; Ladakh Urial -50, Hindu Kush LS: Markhor – 3,400, Himalaya LS: Musk Deer, Brown bear} 1.4: Scale 1 [Limited institutional capacities for planning, implementation and monitoring of multiple use landscape as indicated by the capacity development baseline scores for: (i) National level-18; (ii) GB -18; (iii) AJK- 15 (iv) KP - 18] 2.1 3,100,000 [ hectares of snow leopard areas under existing PA network (national parks, sanctuaries, game reserves and community managed conservation areas, but with exception of around 10,000 ha under National Parks and/or CMCAs, other areas lack appropriate management] 2.2: 4 Management plans approved but lack adequate budgetary provisions 2.3 0 [Limited and uncoordinated training programs currently implemented and incidents of wildlife crime in landscapes] 2.3.b:136 3.1 0 [Lack of integrated landscape planning approaches and the current planning process is mostly at sectoral level] 3.2 Scale 1 3.3: Scale 1[8-10Number of incidents of retaliatory killings of snow leopard and other predators to be assessed in Year 1] 4.1 0 4.2 0 4.3:[No concerted efforts at trans-boundary and inter-provincial collaboration exists]

1.1: Scale 2 [Multiple use landscape frameworks agreed with key stakeholders and under review for official approval] 1.2: 3.8 million hectares [3.8 million hectares area of Karakorum Pamir landscape managed effectively through preparation of its management plan, endorsed by the provincial government] 1.3: Scale 2 [Snow leopard population estimates validated and monitoring in 6000 sq. kms and its prey species validated and monitoring in 17 valleys showing stable or improving population trends from the population described in the baseline Snow leopard population estimate at ± 200 individuals in Northern Pakistan with associated species baselines as: Karakoram-Karakoram-Pamir LS: Himalayan Ibex-4,998; Markhor -235;Blue Sheep – 765; Ladakh Urial -51Hindu Kush LS: Markhor – 3,468] 1.4: Scale 2 [medium (11%-35%)] 2.1: 10 [increase by 10 points in METT from the given baseline] 2.2: 5 [5 management and financing plans agreed with local communities and Guidelines for improved management of CMCAs developed and approved by government] 2.3: 70 [(50 forest officials, 20 community members trained)] 3.1: 8 [8 valley conservation plans developed and approved] 3.2: 5% [5% increase in average incomes (9,450 – 10,500 PKR) from sustainable livelihoods, natural resource management and business activities] 3.3: 10% [(i) 10% decrease in livestock lost/year to wildlife (0.54 head/HH/Year), (ii) 10% decrease in retaliatory killings of snow leopard and other predators (7.2-9 per Year)] 4.1: Scale 2 [20% of participating households (20% of households include women beneficiaries) have good awareness of conservation, sustainable natural resource use and wildlife crime prevention benefits 4.2: Not planned in 2020 4.3: 1 [01 effective collaborative inter-provincial agreement initiated and negotiated]

1.1: Scale1 [Landscape Coordination Committees (LCC) for PSLEP were constituted for 88% of its project valleys in Gilgit Baltistan (GB) and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) province. First meeting of LCC was held in KP on 12 March 2020. Notification of LCC in AJ&K was pursued with the Home Dept. Project board (PSC) meeting was convened in February 2020] 1.2: 3.8 million ha [Landscape Management Plan of Karakorum Pamir covering 3,824,500 hectares was updated and being shared with the provincial stakeholders for comments; Development of Landscape Management Plans of Himalaya and Hindu Kush were awarded] 1.3: Scale 1 [Studies were awarded to University of Chitral and Haripur to assess snow leopard occupancy and abundance in the Hindu Kush Landscapes covering ~5,500km² area. Ungulate surveys were undertaken in 50 valleys of GB and KP. Different species reckoned include ibex (5,408), markhor (5,367), Marco Polo Sheep (110), and blue sheep (550)] 1.4: Scale 1 = low (0-25%) [About 150 field staff of the KP (50) and GB (100) wildlife depts were engaged in the on field trainings focusing on wild ungulates.Training in planning, implementation and monitoring of Landscape Management Plans was postponed due to COVID-19 pandemic] 2.1: 5 [Average increase by 5 points in METT from the baseline in 7 National parks (CKNP, Broghil, Quramber, Musk deer, Khunjerab, Gumot and Chitral Gol National Park)] 2.2: 5 [Five Valley Conservation and Development Plans (VCDPs) were drafted and the development of 3 VCDPs were awarded. Conservation tourism plans of Hopper, Rupal and Bagrote valleys in GB were drafted] 2.3: 50 [Equipment for the implementation of SMART (Spatial Monitoring and Reporting Tool) were procured and training plans were developed. Trainings of the 50 wildlife and community guards in wildlife crime monitoring and surveillance is planned in GB, AJK and KP] 3.1: 5 [Valley conservation plans of 5 valleys were drafted] 3.2: 5% [5% increase in average incomes (9,450 – 10,500 PKR) from sustainable livelihoods, natural resource management and business activities] 3.3:0 [Incidences of retaliatory killing of snow leopard in project area were not reported from the project sites except one case in Hopper valley during the reporting period. Reports of the livestock losses to snow leopard and other carnivores in the project sites are being collected in tandem with the livestock vaccination data and would be available in 2021] 4.1: Scale 2 [The social mobilization team of the project interacted with the communities in 17 valleys for identification, initiation and monitoring of activities. Community learning sessions were conducted during the visits reaching ~10% of the participating households] 4.1: 3 [Documentation of Snow leopard Enterprises (SLE) as a best practice was awarded. Development of the 2nd phase of documentary on Conservation Tourism was awarded. National Strategic plan on ecological and socially responsible tourism in the snow leopard landscapes drafted and shared with the MOCC for comments. Conservation Chronicles a biannual Newsletter of SLF was developed and disseminate] 4.2: No target planned in 2020 4.3: 01 [Meetings were held with Ministry of Climate Change (MOCC), Government of Pakistan and WWF-Pakistan to develop a forum for effective inter-provincial collaboration mechanisms for monitoring and surveillance of wildlife and reduce poaching and illegal wildlife trade. Similarly, the matter was raised with the three provinces during meetings. However, this target is linked with the development of the strategy to curb IWT in the snow leopard range. The strategy is being finalized by the project and will be ready for review of the provincial wildlife departments and MOCC in the first half of 2021]

1.1: Scale 3 [high 50%-60% Multiple use landscape frameworks agreed with key stakeholders and under review for official approval] 1.2: 100,000 1.3: Scale 1 (0-1%) [Snow leopard and associated species validated and monitored in 10,000 sq. km showing stable or improving population trends compared to the baseline] 1.4: Scale 3 2.1: Increase by 10 point METT 2.2: 5 2.3: 70 [50 forest and wildlife persons trained and actively enforcing environmentally friendly management practices, 20 community members actively engaged in wildlife crime monitoring and surveillance in community battalions] 3.1: 4 [valley conservation plans developed and 10 approved] 3.2: 6% [increase in av. income from sustainable livelihoods, NRM and business activities] 3.3: 20% [10% decrease in livestock lost/year to wildlife, 10% decrease in retaliatory killings of snow leopard and other predators] 4.1: Scale 3 [26% of participatory households have good awareness of conservation, sustainable natural resource use and wildlife crime prevention benefits] 4.3: 01 [one effective collaborative inter-provincial agreement initiated and negotiated]
The project is temporarily suspended. NO targets planned

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative: 1.1: Extent to which adequate, consistent, reliable and up-to-date data on Pakistan's environment is available. Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 1.2. Extent to which Quality and reliability of environment data is enhanced Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 1.3. A compendium of Pakistan's environmental statistics, with enhanced contents, regularly published. Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 2.1. Existence of an effectively functioning web (EMIS) portal hosting a multitude of data basis from participating organizations Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 2.2. Sustained participation and coordination of relevant organizations for sustainability of EMIS Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 2.3. Existence of Pakistan's State of Environment Report Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 2.4. Existence of Provincial State of Environment Report(s)/Environmental Profile. Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 2.5. Extent to which country reporting against the three multilateral environmental agreements of CBD, UNFCCC and CCD is enhanced Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 2.6. Extent to which Synergy between environmental research, policy, reporting and practice has been effective. Scale used: 1=Do not exist; 2= Exist in a very basic shape; 3= Exist in a basic shape and 4= Exist in an improved shape. 3.1:

comment.narrative: 1.1: Scale 3 2.1: Scale 2 2.2: Scale 2 2.3: Scale 2 2.4: Scale 2 2.5: scale 3 2.6: Scale 3 2.7 Scale 3 3.2: Scale 2 3.3. Scale 2

1.1: Scale 3 (Repository for environmental data developed & indicators finalized to ensure adequate and reliable environmental data). 2.1: Scale 2 (technical, institutional & IT frameworks developed as well as collaboration with GLOF-II project established to develop EIMS portal). 2.2: Scale 2 (To establish an effective coordination and management structure practical engagement with relevant stakeholders has been carried out. From the government level, P&D Department has the pristine role in sustenance and functioning of EIMS. Focal persons were nominated from Punjab, KP and PBS and active liaison is in place. Academia and youth engaged for major activities. This network of government (P &D and Environment wing, MoCC), PBS, and academia is being strengthened and capacitated in 2020. 2.3: Scale 2 (Research study carried out and in coordination with DG-MoCC, a uniform template for State of Environment report prepared. Consultation with Pak-EPA for template and production of Pakistan SOE is being carried out). 2.4: Scale 2 (Research study carried out and in coordination with DG-MoCC, a uniform template for provincial State of Environment report prepared. Initial consensus for production/updation SOE-Punjab is built with P&D Board-Punjab and for SOE-KP/Environmental profile-KP consensus developed with EPA-Peshawar) 2.6: Scale 3 (Research cooperation between the Ministry of Climate Change (MoCC) and universities was developed through GEB project. Accordingly, LOA was signed between the University of Peshawar (UOP) and MoCC through GEB project. In 2020, in consultation with MoCC, research agenda is being formulated that will be implemented by the UOP. GEB project is also striving to develop linkages for research cooperation with other universities especially in Punjab in 2020. Students and academia engaged for all EIMS related research and capacity building activities). 3.1: Scale 2 (5 trainings/workshops conducted engaging 200 participants (53% females) from relevant departments/stakeholders (PBS, P&D Board-Punjab, EPA-KP, academic universities, PITB, Forest Department etc.). 3.2: Scale 2 linkages built with selected universities for environmental data and research; LoA signed with University of Peshawar; Officials/stakeholders engaged for capacity building 3.3: Scale 3 10 Knowledge products produced for formulation of informed public opinion. GGEB Project website deployed to disseminate information .

1.1: No target planned for 2021 1.2: No target planned for 2021 1.3: Scale 4 2.1: Scale 4 2.2: Scale 3 2.3: Scale 4 2.4: Scale 4 2.5: Scale 3 2.6: Scale 4 3.1: Scale 3 3.2: Scale 3 3.3: Scale 4 3.4: Scale 3

1.3: Scale 4 [GEB project established collaboration with Pakistan Bureau of Statistics (PBS) and Compendium of Environmental Statistics-2020 has been published jointly by GGEB & PBS. Moreover, a detailed study for “identification of frailties in data collection & suggestion for content enhancement of compendium of environmental statistics” is underway. The completion study will lead to the achievement of the target by the end of project life]. 2.1: Scale 4 [The project has advertised detailed RFP for “Software development & operationalization of EIMS” & RFQ for Supply of hardware requisite for operationalization of EIMS”. Selected firm has been engaged by GGEB Project and work is in progress. Thus, a functioning EIMS will be established by the end of project life]. 2.2: Scale 3 (A network of government, P&D and Environment wing, MoCC, PBS, and academia has been established and is being strengthened. Meantime the project is also working on EIMS establishment with Pak-EPA with due contribution of all stakeholders, thus the EIM system established with mutual consensus and feedback, and the network developed for EIMS establishment will ensure its sustainability and operationality thereafter). 2.3: Scale 4 [Pakistan State of Environment Report is in finalization by Pak-EPA, once finalized and approved by competent authorities, it will be published. Target is expected to be achieved by end of project life). 2.4: Scale 4 [Consultative meeting with relevant departments (P&D-Punjab & EPA-KP) have resulted on the consensus that State of Environment (SOE) development is a challenging & time-consuming task for the concerned department. Therefore, GEB Project would extend support and produce guidelines for SOE reporting that will be followed by relevant departments to develop Provincial SOE & target is expected to be completed by end of project life]. 2.5: Scale 3 [In pursuance of the above target, a detailed study titled “Analysing Pakistan’s reporting requirements against multilateral agreements (UNFCCC, UNCBD and UNCCD), identifying International best practices and suggesting improvement required in the country reporting” is being carried out. The study will give practical recommendations to the concerned department(s) responsible for country reporting that can then work to improve the reporting. Thus, the target will be achieved by end of project life]. 2.6: Scale 4 [LOA has been signed with University of Peshawar & research agenda has been formulated. As per the target, 12 research assignments on EIMS related topics are being carried out by engaging students and thus the target will be achieved by end of year 2021. 3.1: Scale 3 [As per approved AWP-2021, 4 workshops on environment/EIMS related topics have been carried and 4 are in planning. 100 government officials and numerous other stakeholders have been engaged. Target is expected to be met by end of project life]. 3.2: Scale 3 [GEB project has adopted a market based approach to policy research and analysis in which the government, on one hand, acts as a supplier of information made possible by the unified data collection and reporting system - EIMS, and on the other, serves as the market for policy research and analysis. Accordingly, GEB project is capacitating relevant stakeholders to increase their environmental expertise as well as establishing EIMS that will thus support them in informed decision making and target will be achieved by end of project life]. 3.3: Scale 3 [An important medium/tool for the formation of “Public Opinion” is knowledge products. To carry out the activity, GEB project has drafted brochure, magazine, factsheets and reports as knowledge products which are now under approval at MoCC. Moreover in concurrence with the target, story boards for 2 documentaries/dramas are in preparation/finalization that will lead to successful achievement of target by end of project life]. 3.4: Scale 3 [One workshop and one site visit has been conducted. Outreach, advocacy and communication strategy is being prepared. 3 op-eds on environment related topics are prepared and published. Target is expected to be achieved by end of project life).
1.1: (No Target for 2022) 1.2: (No Target for 2022) 1.3: Scale 4 2.1. Scale 4 2.2. No target for 2022 2.3. Scale4 2.4. Scale 4 2.5. Scale 4 2.6. Scale 4

description.narrative:
1.1: Extent to which SDGs integrated in existing and new institutional structures Scale: 1. Not at all- SDGs not integrated in the mandate of institutional structures, 2. Very Partially- SDGs very partially integrated in the mandate of institutional structures, 3. Partially- SDGs partially integrated in the mandate of institutional structures, 4.Fully- SDGs integrated in all new and existing institutional structures
1.2: Extent to which SDGs frameworks at national/subnational level are developed and planning tools are SDG aligned.
Scale: 1.Not adequately (No SDG frameworks at national/subnational level are in place), 2. Partially- (SDG frameworks at national/sub-national level established but planning tools are not SDGs aligned), 3. Largely- (SDG frameworks at national/sub-national level established and planning tools are largely substantially SDGs aligned)
1.3: Extent to which key stakeholders have enhanced awareness and understanding of SDGs
Scale: 1. Not adequate (Stakeholders have limited understanding of SDGs), 2. Partial (Stakeholders have very basic understanding of SDGs), 3. Moderate (Stakeholders have decent understanding of SDGs) 4. Significant (Stakeholders have sound understanding of SDGs)
1.4: Extent to which private sector is engaged in the implementation of SDGs
Scale: 1. Not adequately- (Private sector not adequately engaged in SDG implementation), 2. Very Partially- (Private sector very partially engaged in SDG implementation), 3. Partially- Private sector partially engaged in SDG implementation) 4.Fully- Private sector fully engaged in SDG implementation)
1.5: Extent to which Parliament is engaged in the implementation of SDGs
Scale: 1. Not at all- (Parliament not at all engaged in SDG implementation), 2. Very Partially- (Parliament very partially engaged in SDG implementation), 3. Partially- Parliament partially engaged in SDG implementation), 4.Fully- Parliament fully engaged in SDG implementation)

1.1. Scale-2- Very Partially (SDGs integrated in the mandate of existing coordination structures, but no new structures established) 1.2. Scale-2- Partially (SDG frameworks at national/sub-national level established but planning tools are not SDGs aligned) 1.3-Scale-3- Moderate: (Stakeholders have decent understanding of SDGs) 1.4-Scale-2 - Very Partially- (Private sector very partially engaged in SDG implementation) 2.1- Scale-3- Partially (Baselines and targets established for prioritized SDG indicators) 2.2-Scale-2- Very Partially (Little understanding and knowledge to effectively report and monitor progress on SDGs) 3.1-Scale-2 – Partially (Performance based criteria established but not fully used for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level) 3.2-Scale-2 – Partially (frameworks/MIS capacity established for effective aid coordination) 3.3-Scale-2 – Partially (Results based, and inclusive processes partially inform budget allocation at national and sub national level) 4.1-Scale-2 – Partially (Innovative approaches fully established for SDG acceleration)

1.1: Scale-4: Largely 1.2: Scale-3-Partially 1.3: Scale-3-Moderate 1.4: Scale-3 Partially
2.1: Scale-3-Partially 2.2: Scale-3 -Partially
3.1: Scale-3-Fully 3.3: Scale-2-Partially
4.1: Scale-2-Partially

1.1: scale 4 (Largely) to not only embed SDGs in the newly created institutional structures of government/ministries but also to operationalize new institutional structures specifically established for SDGs including NEC-SUB Committee on SDGs for strategic oversight, enhanced policy coherence and sustained horizontal and vertical coordination 1.2: Scale 3 (Partially), National SDG framework has been developed, approved (by NEC) and development plans and policies at national level are partially SDGs aligned. But development and finalization of SDGs frameworks at regional level (GB and AJK) is in progress and not yet finalized therefore the target is set at scale 1.3: scale 3 (Moderate) to raise awareness and enhance the knowledge of more than 50% of government and non-government stakeholders about SDGs at the national and regional level (GB & AJK) through targeted outreach, advocacy and awareness raising activities. 1.4: scale 3 (Partially) to partially engage private sector in the implementation of SDGs through different innovative ways.
2.1: scale 3 (Partially) to update data gap study and to finalize baseline and targets for all priority SDG indicators. The M&E framework, evaluation handbook and other relevant deliverables will also be completed in 2021 though not reflected here in this AWP as it is being implemented with the financial support of UNICEF and its workplan for 2021-2022 is in revision process. 2.2: scale 3 (Partially) to build the capacity of PBS and regional bureaus of statistics to enable them to have reasonable knowledge and understanding on SDGs monitoring and reporting through formal trainings, provision of equipment’s and other institutional/technical support.
3.1: Scale 3 (Fully)Performance based criteria fully implemented for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level 3.3: Scale 2 (Partially) Results- based and inclusive processes partially inform budget allocation at national and subnational level
4.1: scale 2 (Partially) and many innovative activities has been planned to achieve the target.

1.1: Scale-4- Largely 1.2: Scale-3- Partially 1.3: Scale-4-Significant 1.4: Scale-4-Largely 2.1: Scale-3-Partially 2.2: Scale-4 -Largely
3.1: Scale-3-Fully 3.3: Scale-2-Partially
4.1: Scale-2-Partially

1.1: Scale-4 [NEC sub-committee meetings were facilitated with a major focus on strengthening monitoring and reporting on SDGs and prompting coherence] 1.2: Scale-3 [GB and AJK SDG frameworks developed. Policy research was published on Accelerating SDGs Implementation by Reinvigorating Local Governance at the Lowest Tier’ to address local issues related to education, health, water, sanitation and local infrastructure]. 1.3: Scale-4 [Significant Advocacy] 1.4: Scale-4 [SDG investment Report was prepared and launched. An SDG investment facility established to generate private sector financing for SDGs].
2.1: Scale-3 [This first-ever national SDGs report provides a much-needed baseline and progress overview on 133 indicators for the effective monitoring and tracking of SDGs was published along with SDG status reports for GB and PAK. Pakistan also presented it 2nd Voluntary National Report along with SDG twinning with Sri Lanka] 2.2: Scale-4 [To build the capacity of regional bureaus of statistics in GB and PAK and to enable them to have reasonable knowledge and understanding on SDGs monitoring and reporting through formal trainings and other institutional/technical support].
3.1: Scale-3 [No major results in 2022] 3.3: Scale-2 [No major results in 2022]
4.1: Scale-2 [No major results in 2022]

4.1: Scale-3-Fully

1.1: Scale 4 [All new and existing platforms made operational and utilized for policy coherence and sustained coordination. Meetings of these platforms were convened and stakeholder were informed on SDG implementation in the Province]. 1.2: Scale 4 [SDG Action plan/frameworks at national/sub-national level established and development plans are partially SDGs aligned). Sectoral strategies were envisioned in 2022, however these could not be accomplished. The project focused on supporting South Punjab in the context of regional equalization and prepared SDG aligned regional equalization strategy for South Punjab]. 1.3: Scale 3 [Extensive and in-depth understanding of SDGs amongst 50% or more of government and non-government stakeholders). SDG advocacy events were held and SDG advocacy material was extensively disseminated. Capacity-building sessions were held with provincial P&DD and other stakeholders on integrating SDGs into Punjabs planning and budgetary processes and Gender-Responsive Budgeting. These sessions aimed to create an enabling space for policy dialogue and collaboration among provincial line departments to support the ADP (2022) formulation process Civil society was mobilized in collaboration with MilKar]. 1.4. Scale 3 [Private sector partially engaged in SDG implementation). No target was envisioned in 2022.
2.1: Scale 4 [Tools developed to collect data and report progress, capacity building of provincial Bureau of Statistics was continued to ensure monitoring and reporting on SDGs. The project produced district SDG profiles for SDG reporting and SDG planning at district level]. 2.2. Scale 4 [Complete understanding and technical skills to monitor and report SDGs, capacity building of provincial Bureau of Statistics (BoS) was continued including on STATA to ensure monitoring and reporting on SDGs]. 3.1: Scale 3 [Performance based criteria fully implemented for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level). In 2022, the project launched newly developed Integrated Financing Strategy (IFS) for Punjab, which aims to bridge existing financing gaps for SDGs through innovating financing models]. 3.2: scale 2 [Frameworks /MIS capacity established for effective aid coordination). No target was set for 2022. 3.3: scale 3 [Results based, and inclusive processes fully inform budget allocation at national and sub-national level] No target was set for 2022. 4.1: scale 3 [Innovative approaches fully established for SDG acceleration). No activity initiated under this result in 2022.

1.1 scale 3-Partially (51-75%): SDGs integrated in the mandate of existing coordination structures and new structures established 1.2: scale 4-Largely (75% and above): SDG Action plan/frameworks at national/sub-national level established and development plans are partially SDGs aligned 1.3: Scale 4: Stakeholders have decent understanding of SDGs. 1.4: Scale 3 - Private sector partially engaged in SDG implementation. 2.1: Scale-3-Partially (51-75%): Baselines and targets established for prioritized SDG indicators 2.2: scale 3-Partially (51-75%): Reasonable understanding and knowledge on SDGs monitoring and reporting 3.1: scale 2-Partially: Performance based criteria established but not fully used for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level. 4.1Scale-2-Partially: Innovative approaches partially developed for SDGs acceleration.

3.1: Scale-3-Largely
4.1: Scale-3-Largely

1.1 scale 4-[SDGs integrated in the mandate of existing coordination structures and new structures established including NEC sub-committee on SDGs. The project reported regularly to provide updates to NEC Sub-committee on SDGs] 1.2: Scale 4- [SDG Action plan/frameworks at national/sub-national level established and development plans are partially SDGs aligned. 7 publications related to SDGs were disseminated facilitating alignment of provincial strategies with SDGs including Balochistan Comprehensive Development Growth Strategy (2021-2026); SDGs and Islam; Green Building and Design Standards; SDG Costing for Balochistan; Provincial SDG Framework for Balochistan; and District Localization Plans for Nushki and Killa Abdullah]. 1.3: Scale 4: [Stakeholders have a greater understanding of SDGs, the project undertook advocacy around SDGs through district SDG awareness sessions engaging district administration and local stakeholders around SDGs, engagement with academia, and other key stakeholders including provincial commission on status of women. The project also conducted radio shows for SDG advocacy]. 1.4: Scale 3 [Private sector partially engaged in SDG implementation. The project engaged with BOI and introduced it to SDG Investments & Climate Financing Facility]. 2.1: Scale-3-P [Baselines and targets established for prioritized SDG indicators]. 2.2: scale 3-P [Reasonable understanding and knowledge on SDGs monitoring and reporting. In 2022, the provincial BoS and P&DD participated in workshops on knowledge sharing on monitoring and reporting on SDGs. For 2023, in the next phase of the project, capacity building on BoS will be further strengthened on SDG monitoring and reporting]. 3.1: Scale 2-P [Performance based criteria established but not fully used for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level]. 4.1 Scale-3- [Innovative approaches were fully established for SDG implementation. The project in 2022 provided e-commerce training to 100 students (including 30 women),and organized a challenge cup to promote innovation on SDG implementation amongst university students].

1.1: Scale 4 Fully- All new and existing platforms made operational and utilized for policy coherence and sustained coordination 1.2: Scale 4- Largely (75% and above): SDG Action plan/frameworks at national/sub-national level established and development plans are partially SDGs aligned. 1.3: Scale 4 -Significant (75% and above): Extensive and in-depth understanding of SDGs amongst 50% or more of government and non-government stakeholders 1.4:Scale 3-Partially (51-75%)- Private sector partially engaged in SDG implementation) 2.1: Scale 4 Fully (75% and above): Tools developed to collect data and report progress 2.2: Scale 3-Moderate (51-75%): Reasonable understanding and knowledge on SDGs monitoring and reporting 3.1: Scale 3 Fully (75-100%) Performance based criteria fully implemented for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level 4.1: Scale 3-Largely (75% and above): Innovative approaches fully established for SDG
