1.1: Scale 4 [All new and existing platforms made operational and utilized for policy coherence and sustained coordination. In 2022, the project continued strengthening of key capacities of provincial legislators on budgeting, legislation, oversight and policymaking focused on SDGs]. 1.2: Scale 4- [SDG Action plan/frameworks at national/sub-national level established and development plans are partially SDGs aligned. In 2022, the project in collaboration with UNICEF worked on sectoral SDGs including SDG 4 and 6 on education, water, and sanitation sectors and supported Sindh Government on Karachi Master Plan to build an evidence-base for preparing policy papers on SDG localization]. 1.3: Scale 4 -[Extensive and in-depth understanding of SDGs amongst 50% or more of government and non-government stakeholders]. 1.4: Scale 3- [Private sector partially engaged in SDG implementation). In 2022, the project reached out and engaged private actors including Soorty Enterprises, Artistic Milliners, Toyota Indus Motors, and Vlektra, to explore partnership avenues focused on aligning their sustainability strategies, targets, and actions toward Agenda 2030. The project partnered with Pakistan Cables and Muslim Hands to promote urban resilience interventions in informal settlements].
2.1: Scale 4 [Tools developed to collect data and report progress. In 2022, the project developed a results-based management framework for the SDGs which was transferred into a digital portal for SDG tracking in Sindh]. 2.2: Scale 3-[[Reasonable understanding and knowledge on SDGs monitoring and reporting. In 2022, the project organized capacity strengthening sessions for BoS Sindh on SDGs targets, indicators, the corresponding meta -data definitions, and methods of computation and data collection, analysis and reporting].
3.1: Scale 3 [Performance based criteria fully implemented for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level. In 2022, the project prepared a Guidebook on SDGs; a Costing Model for SDG 2: Zero Hunger; a report on Digital Mechanisms for Results Based Management Framework for SDGs; and a report on Financial Tracking for SDGs. The project conducted a review of ADP tagging and analysis for SDGs and tagged Development and Non-Development budgets with SDGs].
4.1: Scale 3-[Innovative approaches fully established for SDG. The project facilitated organizing Challenge Cup to promote urban resilience in informal settlements. The project also completed alignment of 14 HQ towns plans with SDGs. SDGs in Sindh will be completed].

An MIS has been established for BOS Khyber Pakhtunkhwa with the aim to improve reporting of SDGs Indicators. 2.2: Scale 3 (Partially 51-75%): Reasonable understanding and knowledge on SDGs monitoring and reporting has been established by the project since inception. 3.1: Scale 2 (Partially) Performance based criteria established but not fully used for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level. 4.1: Scale 2 (Partially) Innovative approaches partially developed for SDGs acceleration.

1.1: Scale 3 [Up to greater extent SDGs integrated in the mandate of existing coordination structures and new structures established. In 2022, the project continued supporting these structures and in providing periodic updates on provincial SDG progress]. 1.2: Scale 3 [SDG Action plan/frameworks at national/sub-national level established, and development plans are partially SDGs aligned. In 2022, for sub-national planning the project prepared 4 district localization plans for Khyber, Mardan, Upper Dir and Bannu and sectoral policies for 3 priority sectors namely Education, Health and Energy]. 1.3: Scale 3 [Decent understanding regarding the SDGs have been developed over a period. In 2022, the project focused at creating awareness amongst students by organizing awareness sessions at universities, and within public sector through awareness sessions for district officials and for Bureau of Statistics]. 1.4: Scale 3 [No target for 2022]. 2.1: Scale 3 [In 2022, the baselines for more than 100 indicators were updated on BOS Khyber Pakhtunkhwa portal. The project also facilitated review of regional accounts for the estimation of trade sector computation to GDP]. 2.2: Scale 3 [Reasonable understanding and knowledge on SDGs monitoring and reporting has been established by the project since inception]. 3.1: Scale 2 [Performance based criteria established but not fully used for resource allocation at national/ sub-national level. In 2022, the project computed financial cost of achieving High-Priority Short-Term SDG targets in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. 4.1: Scale 2 [Innovative approaches partially developed for SDGs acceleration. No major results in 2022].

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative: 1.1: Strengthened institutional and regulatory systems for climate-responsive planning and development. [Scale: 1= Not adequately (up to 25%); 2= Very partially (26%-50%); 3= Partially (51%-75%); 4= Largely (75%-100%)] 1.2: Number of policies introduced to address GLOF risks or adjusted to incorporate GLOF risks. 2.1: Number of vulnerable households in KP and GB covered by a GLOF early warning system. 2.2: Number of Community-based organisations trained in the operation and maintenance of the EWS. 2.3: Number of physical assets constructed to withstand the effects of GLOF events. 2.4. Number of small-scale community driven initiatives financed through CBDRM funds

comment.narrative: 1.1: Scale 1 (National, provincial and local disaster management institutions and development planners are unable to design, finance and analyze GLOF risk reduction measures on the basis of reliable, comprehensive information.) 1.2: 2 (Only 2 comprehensive disaster management guidelines exist for the KP and GB regions.) 2.1: 0 (Vulnerable households are not able to receive and react to GLOF early warning messages.) 2.2: 2 ( 2 GLOF early warning system for KP and GB in place) 2.3: 0 (No physical structures in place to withstand the effect of GLOF events.) 2.4. 0 (No financial capacity of local communities to prepare and respond to GLOF events)

1.1: Scale 4 [ By the end of Year 3, 100% of the national and 90% of district and community authorities in the KP and GB regions are able to prioritize and plan measures to minimize potential losses from GLOFs] 1.2. 4 [Number of policies introduced to address GLOF risks or adjusted to incorporate GLOF risks] 2.1: 696,342 people [348,171 men, 348,171 women [Planned for end of project]. 2.2: 24 [By the end of the project, at least 24 CBOs are trained in the operation and maintenance of the EWS and ensure its continued functionality]. 2.3: 250 [By the end of the project, at least 250 targeted engineering structures have been established to withstand the effects of GLOF events on livelihood assets]. 2.4: 01 [By the end of the project, at least 01 community driven initiative in each of the 24 target valleys, is financed through CBDRM funds].

1.1: Scale 3 [The project strengthened provincial line departments through experts and technical assistance in GIS mapping, conducting HVRAs and ground-truthing. Climate change cells have been established at national and sub-national level. Initial assessments (Baseline assessment, KAP studies, HVRA etc.) have been carried out. The project helped strengthen the District Emergency Response Cells (DERC) through procurement and provision of emergency and response equipment valued at USD 700,000 to the Disaster Management Authorities in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Gilgit-Baltistan (GBDMA and KPDMA). Components through UNDP support and GOP are on track, however, some activities impacted by COVID]
1.2: 0 [Guidelines and SOPs have been drafted for establishing CBDRMCs Revolving Funds, that will ultimately feed into the developmental plans at provincial and national level to incorporate GLOF risks. CCA action plan for GB has been initiated and is in progress whereas the same process for KP will be initiated in first quarter of 2021]
2.1: 0 [No Early Warning system has been installed yet. Partial procurement of equipment initiated, the procurement and installation of the remaining EWS equipment will be carried out in 2021]
2.2: 0 [12 CBOs (5 in KP and 7 in GB) have been established. Under the project, a series of community meetings, awareness raising campaigns, coordination workshops were conducted to sensitize communities and key stakeholders about the impact of GLOFs. 7 CBOs have had preliminary trainings on the operation and maintenance of EWS. Formal training to CBOs on EWS O&M will be provided once EWS is installed and functional]
2.3: 0 [The groundwork (site selection, design and layout, BoQs) for physical assets has been completed. Construction of most of the infrastructural activities could not be initiated due to COVID-19 restrictions and later on extreme weather conditions, however contracts have been issued against 13 irrigation channels in KP]
2.4: 0 [Guidelines were developed to finance the targeted communities in GB and KP, however, the project was later informed that the activity cannot be implemented as on-granting was not allowed under UNDPs accreditation status with the donor]

1.1: Scale 4 [By the end of Year 3, 100% of the national and 90% of district and community authorities in the KP and GB regions are able to prioritize and plan measures to minimize potential losses from GLOFs] 1.2. 4 [By the end of the project, number of policies introduced to address GLOF risks or adjusted to incorporate GLOF risks] 2.1: 696,342 people [348,171 men, 348,171 women [Planned for end of project]. 2.2: 24 [By the end of the project, at least 24 CBOs are trained in the operation and maintenance of the EWS and ensure its continued functionality]. 2.3: 250 [By the end of the project, at least 250 targeted engineering structures have been established to withstand the effects of GLOF events on livelihood assets]. 2.4: 01 [By the end of the project, at least 01 community driven initiative in each of the 24 target valleys, is financed through CBDRM funds].

1.1: Scale 3 [The strengthening of the institutions and departments both at Federal and Provincial level was done through trainings, workshops, capacity building for human resource, disaster risk reduction and disaster risk management. In 2021, 51 trainings were organized (community and stakeholders) in which 1609 participants attended (1080 men and 529 women), 71 community mobilization sessions with 2169 participants (1598 men and 571 women); 43 mock drills with 2138 participants (1395 men and 743 women) were conducted.] 1.2: 02 [This is Year 04 of the project with an extension until January 2024. The project has so far provided support and inputs for drafting of Climate Change Adaptation Action Plans for GB and KP. These will pave way for devising policy interventions for implementation of Climate Change Policy and Action Plans. The policies to be adopted to address the GLOF risks is linked to the institutional and regulatory systems for climate-responsive planning and development.]. 2.1: 0 [The mapping of households and beneficiaries is available with project for target valleys, Once the EWS is installed, all will benefit from installed EWS.] 2.2: 0 [In all 24 valleys, Community Based Disaster Risk Management Committees (CBDRMCs) have been established. The mandate of these Committees is to implement and safeguard project interventions, specifically focusing on sustainability after project completion. The EWS will be installed in 2022 after which formal training for Operations and Maintenance of EWS to these CBDRMCs will be provided]. 2.3: 28 [In 2021, the GIS mapping and HVRA studies for all 24 project valleys have been completed. 28 engineering structures (irrigation channels) have been completed in 2021 and remaining are aligned for construction in 2022.] 2.4: 0 [Revolving Fund activity is an on-granting activity and formal approval is awaited from GCF on revoking of this activity from project scope].

1.1: Scale 4 [By the end of 2022, 100% (MOCC and PMD) of the national and 100% of district authorities (P&DD, Forest Department, Irrigation department, OFWM, EPA etc.) and communities in KP and GB will be able to prioritize and plan measures to minimize potential losses from GLOFs through capacity building for EWS operation and maintenance and for project interventions] 1.2: 03 [By the end of 2022 at least 03 guidelines/strategy/policy (01 National Policy and 02 Provincial CCAAP) have been established/adopted by the Government to address or incorporate GLOF risk reduction] 2.1: 435,214 people [By the end of 2022, 15 GLOF valleys will have early warning systems installed in KP and GB and 62.5% of households in target communities are able to receive and respond to early warnings and take appropriate actions following the warning (217,607 men, 217,607 women] 2.2: 24 [By the end of 2022, at least 24 CBOs would be trained in the operation and maintenance of the EWS and ensure its continued functionality] 2.3: 200/182/540ha [By the end of 2022, at least at least 200 targeted small-scale infrastructure, 540 Ha of slope stabilization and at least 182 irrigation schemes have been established/rehabilitated to withstand the effects of GLOF events on livelihood assets. 2.4: 0 [Formal approval is awaited from GCF on revoking of revolving fund activity from project scope]

1.1: Scale 3 [The strengthening of the institutions and departments both at Federal and Provincial level was done through capacity building trainings and stakeholder coordination workshops. In 2022, 58 trainings and workshops were completed with 2,340 participants (339 women and 2,001 men). These include - 08 community trainings on CC and GLOF Risk reduction with 338 participants (31 women, 307 men), 27 Mock drills with 1,385 participants (233 women and 810 men), 18 trainings on Climate Change Adaptation Plans (CCAP) with 748 participants (63 women and 685 men) and 05 stakeholder coordination workshops with 211 participants (12 women and 199 men).] 1.2: 02 [02 Climate Change Adaptation Action Plans (01 GB and 01 KP) were approved and adopted by the respective provincial Governments to address the GLOF risks, for integrated institutional and regulatory climate-responsive planning and development. In 2022, Project has also supported the development of 04 plans (02 monsoon and 02 winter contingency plans for GB and KP)]. 2.1: 000 [EWS equipment could not be installed in 2022 due to late land acquisition for base platform construction by the provincial government. Installation will commence in 2023. Once EWS is installed in 2023, the communities will benefit by being able to receive and respond to early warnings.] 2.2: 0 [Trainings of 24 CBOs of the project valleys on operation and maintenance of EWS will be completed in 2023]. 2.3: 67/55/400 [67 small scale infrastructure (against 200), 55 (against 182) irrigation channels and 400 Ha (against 540) slope stabilization activities have been completed and remaining are aligned for construction in 2023]. 2.4: 0 [Decision granted by GCF in November 2022 for revoking of revolving fund activity from project scope. Activity Indicator has been revised in the project log frame approved by GCF in December 2022 and Project activities are aligned for 2023 for alternate livelihood support (kitchen gardening, development of manual for communities and financial institutions for alternate livelihood opportunities)].

title.narrative: SDG investment and Climate Finance Facility

description.narrative: To offer policy guidance and technical support regarding Financing for Development and nature capital solutions to the Government of Pakistan, UNDP Pakistan has set up an SDG Investment and Climate Financing Facility. The objective is to identify, cultivate, and harness high-impact SDG-aligned and climate financing opportunities with the potential to attract investments from domestic and international investors, including financial markets. The project will offer technical assistance and build

1.1: Scale Item 4 2.1 : Scale 3 2.2: 702 VC/NCs, 25 tehsils in 7 districts and 6 sub-divisions 2.3 : 10 % 3.3 : 3( Number of issues identified through research, community and government engagement and resolved at the local level. ) 3.4: 8 ( Special Emphasis Programmes (SEPs) designed and implemented )

1.1. Scale 3 - details below) AIP projects implemented by the Government of KP were appraised with UNDP support on a demand-driven basis, approximately 25 PC-1s. UNDP supported 15 new Planning Officers who were onboarded to support P&D, and trained them on enhanced project appraisal methods for the provincial development portfolio. UNDP trained 115 officials on results-based planning frameworks, called PAMframes, and provided technical assistance to 24 government line departments in the development of sector plans. UNDP also worked with the Planning & Development Department and M&E Directorate to incorporate these planning frameworks in the provincial planning and monitoring and evaluation cycle and support data-driven development. UNDP worked closely with the Planning & Development Department and line departments on the design of high-impact pilot interventions called Special Emphasis Programmes (SEPs), aiming to drive transformative results in sectors that are critical to development in the Merged Areas. 1.2- Extent to which financial accounting and auditing systems are in place in merged areas. Target = No Target (Achieved in 2020) 1.3- Extent to which Governance institutions are strengthened to extend their presence in the merged areas Scale 1 = No capacity Scale 2= human resources exists and problems driven plan are being designed Scale 3= Human resources integrated and need based plans have been developed and staff has been trained to provide support for reforms implementation. Target 1.3: No Target (Scale 3 Already achieved in 2020) 2.1. All 25 TMAs and 7 ADLGs were supported for civil registration services, improved municipal services delivery, trade registrations and COVID awareness/prevention. 2.2. In process. Consultant firm has been hired and work initiated on the plans. 2.3. DROPPED due to indefinite delay in LG elections 3.1. Target: Achieved in 2020 3.2. (Already reached in 2020)= 3.3. Achieved 2 issues relating to land and mining were analyzed. 3.4: 8 SEPs approved by the government. These SEPs include initiatives on improving school enrollment, saving infant and maternal lives, strengthening service delivery, encouraging skills development, advancing women’s entrepreneurship, and a COVID-WASH campaign for sustainable adherence to COVID-19 preventive SOPs.

1.1 Scale 3 [Most of the structures are aligned to integrate human resource system, problem-driven development plans exist and diagnostic study around issues are conducted; MAGP supported district administrations to strengthen their administrative structures (including planning, procurement processes, and the monitoring and evaluation) for improved service delivery through embedding of 28 technical experts across the seven districts. MAGP also supported 8 Planning Officers (2 female), placed with the Planning & Development, Sustainable Development Unit, Home and Higher Education Departments, in addition to 3 Research Associates (1 female) placed with the Development Solutions Lab, P&D (established with MAGP’s technical assistance). In addition, MAGP worked closely with the P&D to: a) conduct 22 in-depth sectoral assessments of key achievements and gaps in AIP I implementation, which formed the basis for AIP II formulation (also developed and finalized with MAGP’s technical assistance); b) develop a Flood Response Plan for the GoKP, including comprehensive mapping of current damages and medium term resilience requirements and c) conduct a Knowledge, Attitudes and Practice Survey to assess citizens’ risk perceptions and disaster preparedness in order to inform future interventions for risk governance and mitigation in the province Moreover, MAGP supported P&D in design and approval of a unique budgetary framework, known as the Da Khushaale Laara District Packages Program (DPP), that funds district-specific interventions aimed at improving service delivery, basic infrastructure and economic opportunities. A total of 13 plans (PC-Is) were designed and approved with MAGP support. 1.2 No result 1.3 No result 1.4: Scale 3 (Projectization of plans (through PC-Is) to engage citizens in improving service delivery) [The PC-I for the Qabail Led Development Programme (QLDP), an innovative rural development program that seeks to engage citizens at the forefront of development planning in their own communities, was finalized and approved by GoKP in September 2022 following a number of departmental engagements throughout the year. In 2023, the programme will move towards implementation (with technical support from MAGP) to establish basic structures for engaging citizens in improved service delivery].
2.1 No result 2.2: 2 [Development of two master plans for Wana in South Waziristan and Mirali/Miranshah in North Waziristan districts was completed in June, concluding MAGP’s support to GoKP on urban planning and development in the MAs] 2.3: 1308 [women 143, men 1165] 3.1: [No result] 3.2: [No result] 3.3: [No result] 3.4: 8 [Social Emphasis Programs finalized and approved by the GoKP (through PC-1s where needed) and all are in various stages of implementation with technical assistance from MAGP]. 3.5: 21% [The AIP II, developed with MAGP’s technical assistance and approved by the Chief Minister in August reflects a 21% increase in total cost and 15% increase in total schemes as compared to AIP I, signaling the government’s resolve towards innovative, high impact, community-based development. The total plan amounts to PKR 460 billion (USD 2.1 billion) spread over 374 schemes (268 ongoing and 106 new). 3.6: 3,776 [3734 men and 42 women] MAGP worked closely with the Home and Tribal Affairs Departments and respective district administrations to conduct over 26 stakeholder engagements, to explore key challenges and opportunities in enhancing border district governance and management.
4.1 No result 4.2 No result 4.3 No result 4.4 Scale 2 [In Aug-Sep, MAGP held over 40 engagements with a diverse group of stakeholders (approximately 5,000 people), including district administrations, line departments, local traders, business associations, cross-border agencies and local communities to diagnose key challenges and opportunities in strengthening border management in the MAs]. 4.5: Scale 2 [In 2022, MAGP engaged with the provincial and federal governments to identify key border crossings for re-opening, including an in-depth needs assessment and context analysis, engaging with local communities, district administrations and traders. At least one crossing is expected to be commissioned and operationalized in 2023]. 5.1: No result

condition.narrative: UNDP as the Implementing Partner will comply with the policies, procedures and practices of the United Nations Security Management System (UNSMS.)

description.narrative: Output 1: UNDP Country Offices and partners will have increased capability for scanning, sensemaking and experimentation for sustainable development solutions in 60 developing countries 1.1 Number of new data sources used at Country Office level: 1.2 Diversity of different data sources used: 1.3 Number of sustainable development solutions identified and documented: 1.4 Variety of innovation methods used to test and iterate over the different hypotheses: Output 2: New sustainable development solutions will be scaled at country level as part of UNDP’s country programme and operations, national policy and/or local markets. 2.1 Learning the Accelerator Lab Network shares with a public audience (outside of UNDP) 2.2 Number of lab insights, processes or products which are taken up by governments or other partners 2.3 Proportion of partners and governments that increase their take up of the innovation methods included in the Accelerator Lab Network suite 2.4 Proportion of UNDP Country Office programming influenced by the Accelerator Lab Network’s work and methodologies Output 3: A core global level result will be that a global learning and scaling network is established and functioning. 3.1 Size and density of the network effects among the Accelerator Labs 3.2 Number of new models, approaches, and insights documented and disseminated throughout the network 3.3 Engagement of the broader Development ecosystem with published learnings from the Accelerator Lab Network 3.4 Amount of influence the learnings and methods of the Accelerator Lab Networks have on the discourse of the broader Development ecosystem.

comment.narrative:
Output 1: UNDP Country Offices and partners will have increased capability for scanning, sensemaking and experimentation for sustainable development solutions in 60 developing countries 1.1 Number of new data sources used at Country Office level: 5 new sources of data per year 1.2 Diversity of different data sources used: 6 different type of data sources per year 1.3 Number of sustainable development solutions identified and documented: 35 new potential solutions per lab per year 1.4 Variety of innovation methods used to test and iterate over the different hypotheses: 5 innovation methods per Action Plan
Output 2: New sustainable development solutions will be scaled at country level as part of UNDP’s country programme and operations, national policy and/or local markets. 2.1 Learning the Accelerator Lab Network shares with a public audience (outside of UNDP): 3 blogs, or other (substantial) media posts published per year. 2.2 Number of lab insights, processes or products which are taken up by governments or other partners: At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partner as counterpart. 2.3 Proportion of partners and governments that increase their take up of the innovation methods included in the Accelerator Lab Network suite: At least one government entity or partner has learned about Accelerator lab methods. 2.4 Proportion of UNDP Country Office programming influenced by the Accelerator Lab Network’s work and methodologies: At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects.
Output 3: A core global level result will be that a global learning and scaling network is established and functioning. 3.1 Size and density of the network effects among the Accelerator Labs: The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network. 48 interaction per year 3.2 Number of new models, approaches, and insights documented and disseminated throughout the network: 25% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 Engagement of the broader Development ecosystem with published learnings from the Accelerator Lab Network: Target: The Accelerator Lab Network´s work is featured at least 3 times per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. The Lab team engages at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account.
3.4 Amount of influence the learnings and methods of the Accelerator Lab Networks have on the discourse of the broader Development ecosystem.
Targets: Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. Identify and engage with 5 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

Output 1: UNDP Country Offices and partners will have increased capability for scanning, sensemaking and experimentation for sustainable development solutions in 60 developing countries 1.1 Number of new data sources used at Country Office level. • Digital listening- conducting interviews using SMS in Gilgit Baltistan for Social Innovation Platform portfolio (SIP) • Survey results for SIPPS • Social media and digital media analysis to identify trends and patterns of social & economic situation of population groups in Gilgit Baltistan. • Used Collective intelligence to map people perceptions and experience of the current systems I.e. future of work, plastics, rule of law programme • Using Systemic design, ethnographic, current, future state mapping exercise research tools for plastics waste management in Pakistan
1.2 Diversity of different data sources used • Social media data, mobile phone data (IVR, Blast SMS) • Crowd sourcing data • Survey data • Qualitative data – interviews • Ethnography data 1.3 Number of sustainable development solutions identified and documented • More than 50 new potential solutions identified for COVID-19 response in Pakistan (Platform link: https://solutionsmapping.wixsite.com/pakistaninnovations ) • 10 new potential solutions identified for plastics waste management in Pakistan link: https://www.pk.undp.org/content/pakistan/en/home/blog/2020/the-good--the-bad-and-the-ugly-of-plastics-in-pakistan.html • Conducted Solutionsfest to identify and protype 10+ solutions for managing plastic waste. • Link: https://undp-pakistan.exposure.co/why-innovation-acclab-pakistan-brought-designers-startups-and-citizens-to-build-solutions-for-plastic-waste
1.4 Variety of innovation methods used to test and iterate over the different hypotheses. • Foresight (stress test), Horizon scanning, Sensemaking, Ethnography, Social Media Analysis, Social Network Analysis, Visual Thinking, Co-creation, Design Sprints/ Innovation challenges, Hackathons, Solutions Mapping, (Community) Asset Mapping, Prototyping, Behavioural Insights, Systems Thinking, Design Thinking, Human Centered Design, Participatory Design, Crowdsourcing, Open Data, Mobile phone data, Portfolio logic, Social Innovation platform approach, Predictive analytics, Sentiment analysis, lanuguage analysis, innovation clinics, systemic design, Collective Intelligence, weak signals of change
Output 2: New sustainable development solutions will be scaled at country level as part of UNDP’s country programme and operations, national policy and/or local markets. 2.1 Learning the Accelerator Lab Network shares with a public audience (outside of UNDP) • 10 blogs published in 2020 • 5 Press Releases published & shared with national media, links below: - https://www.pk.undp.org/content/pakistan/en/home/presscenter/pressreleases/2020/prime-minister-of-pakistan-and-undp-commit-to-accelerate-pakista.html - https://www.pk.undp.org/content/pakistan/en/home/presscenter/pressreleases/2020/undp-and-jazz-to-work-together-for-promoting-youth-led-social-in.html - https://www.pk.undp.org/content/pakistan/en/home/presscenter/pressreleases/2020/undp-and-unilever-pakistan-to-work-together-to-make-rahim-yar-kh.html - https://www.pk.undp.org/content/pakistan/en/home/presscenter/pressreleases/2020/president-of-pakistan-and-undp-to-work-together-on-extending-pak.html - https://www.pk.undp.org/content/pakistan/en/home/presscenter/pressreleases/2020/undp-supports-the-national-health-hackathon-for-local-health-car.html
2.2 Number of lab insights, processes or products which are taken up by governments or other partners • 50%. 1 out of 2 learning challenges has private sector onboard. • Plastic portfolio has Unilever as partner. MoU signed. Ministry of Climate Change has expressed interest in the portfolio. • Social Innovation Platform is in initial digital listening phase coordinating with local civil society and UNDP project. Not yet at partnership stage.
2.3 Proportion of partners and governments that increase their take up of the innovation methods included in the Accelerator Lab Network suite • Unilever adapted systemic design and portfolio design as a method for future programming. • Ministry of Health and Digital Pakistan adopted behaviour insights
2.4 Proportion of UNDP Country Office programming influenced by the Accelerator Lab Network’s work and methodologies;
• 100% - All learning portfolios aim is to produce a proposed and tested portfolio of experiments that are scalable and can be taken up by projects and partners. All learning challenges align with CO unit workplans. Systemic work transitioned from insights to a portfolio of experiments for plastics and SIP; o Systemic design workshop held with Unilever on plastic waste, journey mapping and ethnographic mission, solution fest and crowdsourcing on plastic waste has all resulted in developing a portfolio of experiments on plastic waste management with Unilever and anchored with CO project at the Environment Climate Change Unit. Intelligence report produced and portfolio designed and presented. Link: https://www.pk.undp.org/content/pakistan/en/home/blog/2020/untying-a-socially-complex-problem--one-knot-at-a-time.html o Social Innovation Platform portfolio focusing on socio-economic dynamics post-covid in Hushey Valley has produced a proposed portfolio on gastronomy as a result of the deep listening phase. Next stage is to stress-test the portfolio for scale and implementation. Blog published. Link: https://www.pk.undp.org/content/pakistan/en/home/blog/2020/covid-19--why-we-need-to-listen-to-communities-more-than-ever.html • Conducted a horizon-scan and collective intelligence session to explore future of work. Intelligence report for the youth team produced and the findings from these reports eventually translated into developing a green jobs portfolio. Green jobs was proposed as project in the RFF which CO won. Output 3: A core global level result will be that a global learning and scaling network is established and functioning. 3.2 Number of new models, approaches, and insights documented and disseminated throughout the network. • Lab conducted more than 10 meetings with other AccLabs for sharing portfolio design and systems design work. Lab was also invited twice by regional and global UNDP office for sharing the success of lab’s learning portfolios. (100% information shared) 3.3 Engagement of the broader Development ecosystem with published learnings from the Accelerator Lab Network. • Acclab is featured more than 4 times each month during 2020. • 41 tweets from UNDP’s official account highlighting Lab’s • Lab team engages through linkedin. Facebook, Instagram. 3.4 Amount of influence the learnings and methods of the Accelerator Lab Networks have on the discourse of the broader Development ecosystem. • Connected to local development ecosystem through social media i.e. weak signals of change blog was very well taken up by local government partners (Digital Pakistan) • Engaged with more than 10 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem i.e. political champions, startup founders, other ecosystem players

Output 1: UNDP Country Offices and partners will have increased capability for scanning, sensemaking and experimentation for sustainable development solutions in 60 developing countries 1.1 Number of new data sources used at Country Office level: 10 new sources of data per year 1.2 Diversity of different data sources used: 9 different type of data sources per year 1.3 Number of sustainable development solutions identified and documented: 50 new potential solutions per lab per year 1.4 Variety of innovation methods used to test and iterate over the different hypotheses: 10 innovation methods per Action Plan
Output 2: New sustainable development solutions will be scaled at country level as part of UNDP’s country programme and operations, national policy and/or local markets. 2.1 Learning the Accelerator Lab Network shares with a public audience (outside of UNDP): 8 blogs, or other (substantial) media posts published per year. 2.2 Number of lab insights, processes or products which are taken up by governments or other partners: At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partner as counterpart. 2.3 Proportion of partners and governments that increase their take up of the innovation methods included in the Accelerator Lab Network suite: At least one government entity or partner has learned about Accelerator lab methods. 2.4 Proportion of UNDP Country Office programming influenced by the Accelerator Lab Network’s work and methodologies: At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects.
Output 3: A core global level result will be that a global learning and scaling network is established and functioning. 3.1 Size and density of the network effects among the Accelerator Labs: The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network. 48 interaction per year 3.2 Number of new models, approaches, and insights documented and disseminated throughout the network: 50% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. 3.3 Engagement of the broader Development ecosystem with published learnings from the Accelerator Lab Network: Target: The Accelerator Lab Network´s work is featured at least 3 times per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. The Lab team engages at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account.
3.4 Amount of influence the learnings and methods of the Accelerator Lab Networks have on the discourse of the broader Development ecosystem.
Targets: Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. Identify and engage with 10 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

description.narrative: 1.1 Number of climate monitoring stations in glacier region established and maintained to provide data on changing climate patterns for use by MET Deptt, GB Government (GB-EPA) and other concerned. 1.2 Number of glacial bodies measured, monitored and documented to monitor changes over time that can provide early warning for natural hazards and contribute in reducing risks faced by mountain communities, particularly women and marginalized groups, in downstream valleys. 2.1 Web based GIS climate and glaciers data archiving and sharing system developed and maintained to enable knowledge and data sharing among international institutions for scientific purposes, and the provincial and national authorities e.g. Met Deptt, GB-EPA, Water and Power Department GB, Disaster Management Authorities, Agriculture Deptt, and Water Mgt. Deptt. 2.2 Number of students and faculty members of the local universities in Gilgit-Baltistan trained in glacier inventory and monitoring

1.1: 05 [03 new Automatic Weather Stations are installed/established; and 02 existing Automatic Weather Stations are improved] 1.2: 02 [Glacier bodies identified, surveyed and monitored [Preliminary surveys will be carried out to define the monitoring and research plan. Field activities will be imitated in the selected glaciers. Gender disaggregated Data from the communities will be collected and reported, particularly women and marginalized groups, in downstream valleys] 2.1: 0 [Identification of sites for assessment and measurements, methodology and work planning] 2.2: 0 [The information system will be interoperable and will follow the international standards in terms of metadata and data format and QA/QC procedure, in order to guarantee the reliability of the information, facilitating the exchange of information at different level]

1.1: 05 [03 new Automatic Weather Stations are installed/established; and 02 existing Automatic Weather Stations have been improved] 1.2: 02 [Glacier bodies identified, surveyed and monitored [Preliminary surveys are carried out to define the monitoring and research plan. description.narrative: Since June 2022, Pakistan has experienced unprecedented monsoon rainfall, equivalent to 2.9 times the national 30-year average. This has caused widespread flooding and landslides across the country, with severe repercussions for human lives, property, and infrastructure. According to the latest estimates around 33 million1 people across in all provinces of the country have been affected. Over 436,000 houses have been destroyed and over 736,000 partially damaged. 5,000 km roads, and 243 bridges h

title.narrative: No. of Policies/guideline s/strategies/fram eworks developed for effective implementation of NDCs at national and Provincial level

description.narrative: Under this activity, various policies/guidelines/strategies/frameworks will be developed. Gilgit Baltistan and Baluchistan Climate Change policy will be developed, a financing strategy for Baluchistan will also be developed under this activity through extensive collaboration and consultation with key stakeholders and relevant programme units at UNDP. Moreover, Gender Specific Sectoral analysis report will also be formulated along with draft long-term Low Emission Development Strategy (LT-LEDS) under the Lt-LEDS process.

title.narrative: No. of persons from vulnerable and marginalized groups including women, PWDs, Religious minorities, youth and transgender reached out through skill development training programmes on green skills

description.narrative: The component will focus on gender inclusion and will provide skill development trainings to vulnerable groups in green economy. The activity will include training of 3000 youth in green skills and 1000 young women on ecotourism and livelihood opportunities.

title.narrative: Business centers/career launchpads developed at local and academic level to promote green careers

description.narrative: The activity focuses on development of career launchpads and business centers in Gilgit Baltistan and establishment of green launchpads in universities to support the youth in perusing the green careers through awareness programs and activities.

Under this activity, the team will be developing various knowledge products and organize events to showcase the progress against all thematic areas under NDC implementation. One road show will be organized to showcase the energy efficient technologies. An adaptation report will be developed to be presented in COP28 and COP29. Success stories, documentaries and animations will be developed on the progress against the activities in the specified quarters.

title.narrative: People reached out to enhance awareness on better solid waste management practices

description.narrative: The activity will focus on awareness activities and engaging the public for better solid waste management activities. This will include awareness at the household level, regional level and at the local department level.

title.narrative: Extent to which Climate Finance systems are established at selected Federal and Provincial government departments

description.narrative: The activity will mainstream climate change into the planning and budgeting systems. The climate budget tagging system will be introduced in selected federal/provincial government departments to demonstrate effective climate finance reporting. The Islamic Republic of Pakistan is one of the beneficiary countries of the Global Shield against Climate Risks – a G7 and V20 global initiative to close the financial protection gap in climate vulnerable developing countries. Since the launch of the Global Shield against Climate Risks at COP27 in Sharm El-Sheikh, the global initiative has commissioned in-country processes in 13 countries and the Pacific region to establish gaps in financial protection related to climate, disaster risk finance and insurance (CDRFI).With the formal onboarding of Pakistan as a Global Shield pathfinder country in 2023, the Ministry of Climate Change and Environmental Coordination nominated the United Nations Development Programme to set up the in-country processes in Pakistan. Under the auspices of the Ministry of Climate Change and Environment, UNDP led the stock taking exercise and in close collaboration with in-country stakeholders, establishing the gaps in financial protection and developing the CDRFI request for support.

description.narrative: The SDGs Plus programme will continue putting a premium on Pakistan’s national and sub-national governments as leaders and drivers of the SDGs agenda in Pakistan. It will be anchored in an integrated framework comprising three strategic outputs:a. Mitigate challenges around policy and governance including support on regional equalization in least developed areas, particularly those most affected by 2022 floods; building capacity for transformative governance, reforms, and digitalization; and optimally aligning Public Sector Development Plans (PSDPs) and Annual Development Plans (ADPs) with priority SDGs.b. Support mobilization of innovative Financing for Development; andc. Mainstream Gender, Inclusion and Innovation in national, sub-national and local development processes and solutions.

title.narrative: Strengthening Justice and Security Sector Governance in Pakistan (Rule of Law Programme)

description.narrative: The 2030 Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) 16 and 5 re-affirm that peace, stability, accountable governance, adherence to the rule of law and achieving gender equality and empowering women and girls are essential pre-requisites for sustainable development. Strong and inclusive institutions, good governance, access to justice and security for all, and a stable economy are essential pre-requisites to achieve durable peace, development, and stability in Pakistan. A major factor in the countrys transition to long-term peace, security, stability, and development will be the state’s ability to strengthen justice and security sector governance, and the reasonable delivery of these services to the people of Pakistan, However, Pakistan is at a crossroads, facing a myriad of challenges relating to political instability, armed conflict and violent extremism, weak governance and institutions, lack of inclusive justice and security for all, high vulnerability to climate disasters, and a crisis hit economy with rapidly

The initiative “Global Programme on National Biodiversity Finance Plans” is an enabling activity that directly supports the implementation of the new biodiversity targets under the new Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF), specifically it will focus on providing support to countries in developing: a) a national biodiversity finance policy and institutional review; b) an expenditure review to assesses spending related to biodiversity across all relevant sectors; c) an assessment of financial needs required to achieve the targets of the GBF; and, d) a National Biodiversity Finance Plan. This is underpinned by a transformational process to develop a shared vision and action agenda on financing nature in the country, closely involving the finance sector.

title.narrative: Water for Development (W4D): Support to mountain sustainable development and adaptation policies

description.narrative: The project aims to increase the climate adaptation and resilience of Gilgit Baltistan Province- – which is one of the most fragile in the country from an ecosystem perspective – in order to contribute to the promotion of sustainable and inclusive development in mountainous regions. Despite the high vulnerability to climate change-related risks and the existing national policy and strategy framework, there are still important scientific and technological gaps in the country that do not allow an appropriate definition and management of environmental risk prevention systems, as well as the effective and evidence-based implementation of policies and strategies for sustainable management of natural resources. To meet the challenges of climate change the institutional and community capacity to safeguard the ecosystems and to manage natural resources, especially water resources as determinants of development, has to be increased. More specifically, in line with the priorities of the National Climate Resilience and Adaptation Plan 2023-2030 (NAP),

description.narrative: The objective of the project is to continue strengthening the functioning of the Ozone Cell (National Ozone Unit) within the Ministry of Climate Change as a focal point in Pakistan for all matters relating to the phase-out of Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS) under the Montreal Protocol. The National Ozone Unit will follow up and implement all related activities such as legislative/regulatory measures, data reporting, mass awareness, implementation of HPMP, promotion of alternatives to CFC-based Metered Dose Inhalers and the accelerated phase-out of the Hydrochlorofluorocarbons, etc. This project assists the Government of Pakistan to achieve its long-term goals to implement various ODSs phase-out management plan (HPMP). It aims:· To take necessary measures for the implementation of provisions of the Montreal Protocol.· To process phase-out sub-projects for financial assistance from Multilateral Fund (MLF) through UNIDO;· To coordinate the phase-out activities with UNDP / UNIDO / UNEP and MLF.· To assist the local industry in the preparation of phase-out sub-projects.· To disseminate information on ODSs free technologies and phase out activities.· To oversee and monitor import & usage of ODSs as well as the implementation of phase-out projects.· To establish and maintain a database regarding demand and supply of ODSs in the country

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

title.narrative: Pakistan Legislative, Electoral, Digital Governance and Enpowerment (PLEDGE)

description.narrative: The project is designed to provide technical assistance to Election Commission of Pakistan (ECP), with a focus on promoting more inclusive, credible, and accountable electoral processes. Building on lessons learnt and insights from UNDP’s support to the previous electoral cycles in Pakistan from 2012 to 2024, this initiative is a direct response to the Government of Pakistans request for continued electoral support. It is aligned with the recommendations outlined in key election reports, including the UN Need Assessment Mission (NAM) report (2022 and 2024), the Free and Fair Election Network (FAFEN) report on the 2024 general elections, and the European Union Election Observation Mission (EU EOM) report from the 2018 elections. With the overall goal to support Pakistan in consolidating its democratic governance, the project outcome contributes to a more resilient, inclusive, and transparent electoral environment that strengthens social cohesion, and upholds the principles of human rights, gender equality and sustainable development. This will in turn contribute to the achievement of Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions), SDG 5 (Gender Equality), and SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities).The project aims to strengthen democratic governance and electoral processes in the country through its four outputs of support: 1) institutional capacity of the ECP strengthened through targeted technical support for enhancing public trust on electoral system; 2) enhanced inclusiveness through stakeholder engagement, capacity development, civic and voter education and registration for participative and effective decision-making processes; 3) parliamentary effectiveness and oversight strengthened through technical, advisory and capacity building support for inclusive legislative processes; and 4) enhanced digital information security and inclusiveness in political processes and social environment to ensure safe and responsible digital practices.

title.narrative: Enhancing Climate Adaptive Resilience and Economic Recovery (ECARE)

description.narrative: Pakistan faces intersecting crises of climate-induced disasters, economic instability, and energy insecurity, disproportionately affecting vulnerable populations. The 2022 floods, which impacted over 33 million people and caused nearly USD 30 billion in damages, underscore the urgent need for integrated, resilient solutions.This project addresses these challenges through a transformative, multi-sectoral approach. It is structured around three interlinked outcomes:1. Enhancing anticipatory early warning systems using IoT-based multi-hazard monitoring, real-time data sharing, and culturally responsive communication.2. Supporting inclusive economic empowerment, particularly for women, through targeted livelihood restoration, financial inclusion, and skills development.3. Promoting climate resilience and decentralised renewable energy via climate-smart agriculture, local economic revitalisation, and off-grid energy systems.The project document is the umbrella program, which will serve as the key program for projects and the initiative focusing on enhancing climate resilience through EWS and economic Inclusive recovery, gender empowerment and providing solutions to the energy crisis in Pakistan by transitioning coal with renewable energy options/alternatives. An initial USD 4 million has been secured from the Government of China (CIDCA) for Outcome 1. The ECARE programme will adopt a phased financing model, enabling additional energy and economic empowerment projects to be integrated as funding becomes available. Future resource mobilisation will target bilateral and multilateral donors, the private sector, and international financial institutions. The project will contribute to Pakistan’s national development priorities and international frameworks, including the SDGs—notably SDG 1 (No Poverty), SDG 2 (Zero Hunger), SDG 5 (Gender Equality), SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities), and SDG 13 (Climate Action)—and the Se

The 2025 monsoon floods devastated communities across Pakistan, impacting more than 5.8 million people nationwide. In Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP), flash floods and cloudbursts in Swat and Buner districts destroyed homes, markets, community facilities, irrigation systems, and critical road networks, isolating thousands of households from essential services and livelihoods. In Punjab, the country’s agricultural hub, widespread flooding disrupted cropping cycles, eroded fertile land, and damaged rural infrastructure and irrigation channels, heightening food insecurity and economic vulnerability.

title.narrative: Poverty Reduction through Inclusive Development Program

description.narrative: The Poverty Reduction through Inclusive Development Program (PRIDP) builds on and diversifies the economic empowerment portfolios of the UNDP’s past interventions on economic empowerment through a rights-based approach that gives primacy to expanding the scale and scope of opportunities available to vulnerable groups and populations living in poverty, empowering them to transition to economic wellbeing. The program will apply a gender-transformative approach that not only increases women’s access to economic resources but also addresses underlying structural barriers, such as unequal care burdens, restricted mobility, and limited decision-making power, that perpetuate poverty across generations.

This PPG Initiation Plan, developed by the UNDP Pakistan Country Office with the support from the Bangkok Regional Hub, describes how the project preparation grant from the GEF will be programmed to develop a full project document for the Project Integrated management of land and water resources for strengthening climate resilience in flood affected areas of Balochistan, Pakistan, with supporting documentation for submission to the GEF for CEO endorsement. The Project Information Form (PIF), submitted by UNDP as the GEF Agency, on behalf of the Government of Pakistan was technically cleared by GEF Secretariat and included in the January 2026 Work Programme of the GEF Council.
